proposals.title: A Multi-wavelength Census of the Pulsar Wind Nebula Candidate [Source Name]: Confirming VHE Emission with VERITAS and Characterizing its Broadband Spectrum
proposals.motivation: Pulsar Wind Nebulae (PWNe) are dominant sources of very-high-energy (VHE, >100 GeV) gamma-rays in our Galaxy, serving as laboratories for particle acceleration and non-thermal emission processes. The identification and characterization of new VHE-emitting PWNe are crucial for understanding their population, evolution, and connection to their central pulsars. Pre-2026, the candidate PWN [Source Name] has been identified through X-ray and radio observations, but its VHE nature remains unconfirmed, limiting our understanding of its broadband spectral energy distribution and particle content.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the candidate PWN [Source Name] is a genuine TeV gamma-ray source, and its VHE emission spectrum, when combined with archival X-ray and radio data, will reveal a spectral energy distribution consistent with synchrotron and inverse-Compton models, confirming its PWN nature and constraining the properties of its relativistic particle population.
proposals.method: We will analyze deep, targeted observations of the candidate with the VERITAS imaging atmospheric Cherenkov telescope array, using standard event reconstruction and gamma-hadron separation techniques (e.g., boosted decision trees) to produce a significance map and extract a VHE spectrum. We will cross-match the VHE morphology with archival Chandra/XMM-Newton X-ray and VLA radio maps. We will then perform broadband spectral modeling using a leptonic synchrotron and inverse-Compton model (e.g., naima), fitting to the radio, X-ray, and new TeV data points to derive the nebular magnetic field strength and particle energy distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive VHE detection and the first broadband spectral model for [Source Name], solidifying its classification as a PWN. It will contribute a new, well-characterized object to the growing catalog of TeV PWNe, enabling future population studies. The derived physical parameters will inform models of particle transport and cooling in these systems.
proposals.novelty: While the candidate was known from other wavebands, this proposal leverages Dr. Schneider's expertise in SDSS spectroscopic analysis of AGN to a Galactic context, applying a similar multi-wavelength, spectroscopic mindset to characterize a non-AGN source. The novelty lies in the systematic, model-driven confirmation and physical interpretation of the VHE emission, moving beyond a simple detection.

proposals.title: Searching for Precursor Emission-Line Variability in High-Luminosity AGN: A Time-Lag Analysis with SDSS-V and eROSITA
proposals.motivation: In reverberation mapping, broad emission-line variations lag behind continuum changes due to light travel time from the central engine to the broad-line region (BLR). This paradigm assumes the continuum drives all variability. However, theoretical models suggest instabilities could originate in the accretion disk itself, potentially causing line-emitting regions to respond to changes in the ionizing continuum *before* those changes are fully manifested in the observed optical/UV continuum. Testing this 'inverted' response is crucial for understanding the physical drivers of AGN variability and the structure of the inner accretion flow.
proposals.hypothesis: In a subset of high-luminosity, high-accretion-rate AGN, variability in key broad emission lines (e.g., C IV, Mg II) will exhibit measurable time lags *preceding* corresponding variability in the optical continuum, indicating a physical origin of variability within the inner disk or wind base.
proposals.method: We will construct a sample of high-luminosity (log L_bol > 46) quasars from SDSS-V's Black Hole Mapper with multi-epoch optical spectroscopy and concurrent multi-epoch photometry from the Legacy Survey. For a subset with X-ray detections from eROSITA (eFEDS and wider surveys), we will incorporate X-ray light curves as a proxy for the innermost accretion flow state. Using a modified cross-correlation function (CCF) analysis and stochastic process modeling (e.g., JAVELIN), we will systematically search for statistically significant negative lags (line leading continuum) across the sample, controlling for red-noise characteristics and survey cadence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, observational test for precursor line variability in AGN. A detection would challenge standard reverberation assumptions and point to disk instabilities as the primary driver of variability in some systems. It will also demonstrate a novel use of SDSS-V's multi-epoch spectroscopy combined with multi-wavelength surveys for detailed variability studies.
proposals.novelty: While extensive work pre-2026 has measured positive lags, a dedicated search for statistically robust *negative* lags—where the line leads the continuum—has not been performed. This inverts the standard causal framework of reverberation mapping.

proposals.title: Probing the Luminosity-Variability Relation at the Extremes: A Multi-Epoch Spectroscopic Study of High-Luminosity Quasars from SDSS-V and eROSITA
proposals.motivation: A well-established anti-correlation exists between quasar luminosity and optical variability amplitude, often attributed to the scaling of the accretion disk's thermal timescale. However, this relation is poorly constrained at the highest luminosities (e.g., log L_bol > 47.5 erg/s), where current samples are small and sparse. Testing whether variability saturates or continues to decline at these extremes is crucial for constraining accretion disk physics and the nature of the variability driver.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the luminosity-variability anti-correlation flattens or exhibits a turnover at the highest observed quasar luminosities, indicating a saturation of the characteristic variability timescale or a change in the dominant physical mechanism driving the fluctuations.
proposals.method: We will construct a sample of extremely luminous quasars (z < 3.5) by cross-matching SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper spectroscopy with X-ray selected sources from eROSITA (e.g., eFEDS) and legacy SDSS optical imaging. We will measure spectroscopic variability using multi-epoch SDSS-V spectra, focusing on continuum and broad emission line (e.g., C IV, Mg II) flux changes. The Index Diagram method, developed by Schneider et al. (2025), will be used to robustly separate AGN and host galaxy spectral components, ensuring accurate luminosity estimates. We will then compare the variability amplitude of this extreme-luminosity sample to the well-defined relation from lower-luminosity SDSS quasars.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first robust, spectroscopic measurement of optical variability in a statistically significant sample of quasars at the highest luminosities. It will directly test the predictions of accretion disk models at their physical extremes. The results will inform the interpretation of AGN variability across cosmic time and have implications for understanding the stability of super-Eddington accretion flows.
proposals.novelty: This study leverages the unique, multi-epoch spectroscopic capability of SDSS-V, combined with eROSITA's unbiased X-ray selection, to probe a previously inaccessible parameter space. The application of the Index Diagram ensures that host galaxy contamination, a critical systematic at high luminosity, is definitively removed.

proposals.title: The UV Reverberation Mapping of Low-Luminosity AGNs: A Direct Test of Accretion Physics Scaling
proposals.motivation: Reverberation mapping (RM) campaigns have established the broad-line region (BLR) size-luminosity relation for luminous AGNs, a cornerstone of single-epoch black hole mass estimation. However, it is unknown if this relation holds for low-luminosity AGNs (LLAGNs), where the accretion flow may transition to a radiatively inefficient mode. Testing this scaling is critical for validating the universality of AGN physics and for accurately measuring black hole masses in the local universe, where most supermassive black holes are in low-accretion states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that LLAGNs will follow an extrapolation of the established UV BLR size-luminosity relation, indicating a scale-invariant accretion process. Alternatively, a significant deviation would signal a fundamental change in BLR structure or photoionization at low accretion rates.
proposals.method: We will conduct a dedicated, symmetric UV monitoring campaign using the Hubble Space Telescope's Cosmic Origins Spectrograph (HST/COS) to target a sample of ~15 LLAGNs (L(5100Å) < 10^42 erg/s) selected from the SDSS-IV SPIDERS and SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper catalogs with known redshifts and X-ray counterparts (e.g., from eROSITA/eFEDS). Targets will be observed in the FUV to monitor the C IV and/or Lyα emission lines bi-weekly over an 18-month baseline. We will apply cross-correlation function (CCF) analysis, as used in previous SDSS RM projects, to measure time lags and construct velocity-resolved delay maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic UV RM measurements for LLAGNs, directly testing the scaling of accretion physics into the low-luminosity regime. It will determine if single-epoch mass estimators are valid for these objects and provide crucial empirical data on BLR kinematics and structure as a function of Eddington ratio.
proposals.novelty: While extensive RM exists for luminous quasars, systematic UV monitoring of LLAGNs with HST's sensitivity is unprecedented. This study symmetrically applies the methodology used for high-L AGNs to the low-L regime, enabling a clean test of accretion scaling laws.

proposals.title: Probing the Faint End of the AGN Population: A Test of the Constancy of the Optically Faint Fraction with eROSITA and SDSS-V
proposals.motivation: A key observational result in AGN demographics, established from surveys like SDSS and ROSAT, is that a roughly constant fraction (~15-20%) of X-ray selected AGN are optically faint (i.e., have high X-ray-to-optical flux ratios, log(f_X/f_opt) > 1). This constancy has been interpreted as evidence for a distinct, possibly dust-obscured or intrinsically weak, population. However, this result is based on samples above relatively bright optical flux limits. It is unknown if this fraction holds at fainter optical magnitudes, a regime critical for understanding the complete AGN population and its connection to galaxy evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: The fraction of optically faint AGN among X-ray selected sources is not constant but increases significantly below the current spectroscopic completeness limits of large surveys, revealing a larger population of heavily obscured or low-luminosity AGN.
proposals.method: We will leverage the eROSITA Final Equatorial Depth Survey (eFEDS) X-ray source catalog and the spectroscopic follow-up capabilities of SDSS-V's Black Hole Mapper. For X-ray sources in eFEDS below the traditional SDSS optical spectroscopic limit (r > ~22), we will employ forced-photometry on deep optical/IR imaging (e.g., DES, HSC, Legacy Surveys) to measure precise optical fluxes and colors. We will then use a combination of photometric redshift estimates, spectral energy distribution (SED) fitting with templates including host galaxy contributions, and targeted spectroscopic follow-up of a subsample with SDSS-V's faint-object capabilities to classify sources and measure log(f_X/f_opt). Statistical completeness corrections will be applied to derive the true faint fraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first robust measurement of the optically faint AGN fraction at the faint flux limits probed by eROSITA, directly testing a foundational demographic result. It will quantify the prevalence of heavily obscured AGN missed by optical surveys and inform models of AGN obscuration and the cosmic growth of supermassive black holes.
proposals.novelty: While the constant fraction is a well-known result, its systematic test at the faint optical fluxes now accessible via the eROSITA/SDSS-V synergy has not been performed. This proposal directly challenges an established observational paradigm by pushing into an unexplored parameter space.

proposals.title: Dusty or Distant? A Systematic Search for Heavily Obscured AGN in SDSS-V/eROSITA Sources Lacking I-band Counterparts
proposals.motivation: In optical quasar surveys, the absence of an I-band counterpart is often used as a simple, efficient filter to select high-redshift (z > 6) candidates. This assumption, however, may conflate high-redshift quasars with a potentially significant population of lower-redshift, heavily dust-reddened active galactic nuclei (AGN). Identifying this obscured population is crucial for completing the census of supermassive black hole growth and understanding AGN obscuration mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant fraction of X-ray-selected AGN from surveys like eFEDS that lack I-band optical counterparts are not at high redshift, but are instead lower-redshift systems experiencing extreme dust extinction within their host galaxies or along the line of sight.
proposals.method: We will construct a sample of X-ray sources from the eROSITA Final Equatorial Depth Survey (eFEDS) cross-matched with SDSS-V optical spectroscopy that lack Pan-STARRS or Legacy Survey I-band detections. We will perform detailed spectral energy distribution (SED) modeling from X-ray to mid-infrared (using WISE) wavelengths, employing flexible dust extinction laws (e.g., SMC, Calzetti) and AGN templates. Redshifts will be determined from SDSS spectra, focusing on identifying broad emission lines (e.g., H-alpha, H-beta) redshifted into redder optical bands or via narrow lines. We will compare the derived physical properties (redshift, column density NH, AV) to those of a control sample of I-band-detected eFEDS AGN.
proposals.contribution: This work will quantify the contamination rate of dusty AGN in high-redshift quasar selection filters and identify a population of extremely reddened, low-to-moderate redshift AGN. It will provide empirical constraints on the prevalence and nature of heavy obscuration in the AGN population, informing models of black hole-galaxy co-evolution.
proposals.novelty: While dust-reddened quasars are known, this study systematically targets the specific selection niche (missing I-band) reserved for high-z quasars, leveraging the unique combination of SDSS-V spectroscopy and eROSITA X-ray data to break the redshift-extinction degeneracy in a statistically robust sample.

proposals.title: Constraining the High-Redshift AGN Luminosity Function: A Joint Modeling Approach Using eROSITA X-ray and SDSS-V Optical Surveys
proposals.motivation: The observed number density of luminous AGN at z>6 is surprisingly low, posing a challenge to models of early supermassive black hole growth and reionization. Current constraints from optical/IR surveys like SDSS are limited by small samples and selection biases against obscured or low-luminosity AGN. Joint analysis with wide-area X-ray data (e.g., eROSITA/eFEDS) can break degeneracies in AGN luminosity function models.
proposals.hypothesis: The current low observed number of z>6 AGN is consistent with a luminosity function featuring a steeper faint-end slope and/or a higher normalization than typically assumed, which would be revealed by forward-modeling of multi-wavelength survey selection functions.
proposals.method: We will construct a flexible parametric model for the z>6 AGN X-ray and optical luminosity functions. Using the eFEDS X-ray source catalog and SDSS-V optical quasar samples, we will perform a Bayesian forward simulation of the observed populations. The simulation will incorporate realistic selection functions, redshift-dependent obscuration models, and host galaxy contamination (using the Index Diagram technique from recent SDSS-V work). We will use Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to constrain the model parameters (e.g., break luminosity, faint-end slope, normalization) by comparing simulated and observed source counts and redshift distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first joint X-ray+optical constraints on the z>6 AGN luminosity function, quantifying the allowed parameter space for early black hole growth. It will deliver robust predictions for the AGN population detectable in upcoming deep surveys with JWST and Roman, informing strategies for finding the first generation of quasars.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond simple extrapolations of lower-redshift luminosity functions by directly fitting to multi-wavelength survey data with a full treatment of selection effects, leveraging the unique combination of SDSS-V's spectroscopic depth and eROSITA's wide-area X-ray sensitivity.

proposals.title: The Uniformity of X-ray and Optical AGN Populations: A Comparative Analysis of the Chandra Deep Field South and eFEDS
proposals.motivation: Recent work on X-ray selected AGN from surveys like eROSITA/eFEDS, cross-matched with SDSS spectroscopy, has revealed complex relationships between X-ray properties, broad absorption lines (BALs), and host galaxy contamination. However, it remains unclear whether these findings represent universal AGN population characteristics or are specific to the flux limits and sky area of a particular survey. Testing population uniformity across independent, deep fields is crucial for validating AGN evolutionary models and selection biases.
proposals.hypothesis: The distributions of X-ray hardness ratios, optical spectral properties (e.g., emission line indices, BAL presence), and the correlation between X-ray absorption and optical spectral type for AGN in the Chandra Deep Field South (CDF-S) will be statistically consistent with those derived from the eFEDS sample, indicating a uniform population at these depths.
proposals.method: We will apply the identical analysis pipeline from recent eFEDS/SDSS studies to the 7 Ms Chandra Deep Field South. This involves cross-matching the CDF-S X-ray source catalog with existing deep optical spectroscopic surveys (e.g., VIMOS, MUSE). We will measure X-ray hardness ratios, perform optical spectral decomposition using the Index Diagram method to separate AGN and host galaxy light, and classify BAL features. Statistical comparisons (e.g., Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests) of property distributions between the CDF-S and eFEDS AGN samples will be conducted, controlling for redshift and luminosity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical test of the reproducibility of key AGN population results across independent, deep fields. It will either confirm the universality of the observed X-ray/optical relationships or identify field-to-field variances that must be accounted for in cosmological models. The analysis will also produce a publicly available catalog of uniformly analyzed AGN properties for the CDF-S.
proposals.novelty: While the CDF-S has been studied extensively, a direct, methodologically consistent comparison of its AGN population to the newer, wider-area eFEDS sample to test population uniformity has not been performed. This applies the specific spectral analysis tools (Index Diagram) developed for SDSS-V BHM to a legacy deep field for a controlled experiment.

proposals.title: Revisiting the Abell Catalog's Completeness at Moderate Redshifts: A Multi-Wavelength Cross-Match with SDSS and eROSITA
proposals.motivation: The Abell catalog of rich galaxy clusters has been a cornerstone of extragalactic astronomy for decades, often used as a benchmark for completeness. However, its visual selection on photographic plates is known to be susceptible to projection effects and redshift-dependent biases. Modern, objectively selected cluster catalogs from surveys like the Palomar Distant Cluster Survey (PDCS) have suggested incompleteness in Abell at z > 0.2, but the possibility that the Abell catalog itself suffers from systematic biases at these redshifts has not been rigorously tested with contemporary multi-wavelength data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the canonical Abell catalog is systematically incomplete or biased at moderate redshifts (0.2 < z < 0.4) due to its visual selection function, and that a significant population of rich clusters meeting Abell's criteria are missing, particularly those with dominant AGN or complex morphologies that obscure the galaxy overdensity in optical plates.
proposals.method: We will leverage the spectroscopic and photometric depth of the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) and the X-ray data from the eROSITA Final Equatorial Depth Survey (eFEDS) to construct an independent, multi-wavelength-selected sample of rich clusters in the SDSS footprint. Using optical redshift data and the 'Index Diagram' method to separate AGN and host galaxy light, we will identify cluster cores. We will then perform a positional and richness cross-match between this new sample and the Abell catalog, quantifying the fraction of rich, X-ray luminous clusters absent from Abell as a function of redshift and optical properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative assessment of the Abell catalog's completeness in the modern survey era, crucial for studies that still rely on its historical sample. It will also deliver a new, value-added catalog of moderate-redshift clusters with confirmed AGN activity from SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper spectroscopy, linking cluster evolution to central supermassive black hole growth.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the traditional comparison baseline by testing the established Abell catalog against modern, multi-wavelength data rather than assuming its completeness to validate newer surveys. It uniquely combines SDSS-V's AGN spectral diagnostics with eROSITA's X-ray selection to identify clusters that may have been missed by purely optical, visual inspection.

proposals.title: The Mass Fidelity of PDCS Clusters: A Multi-wavelength Test of Optical/IR Selection as a Mass Proxy
proposals.motivation: The Planck Early Sunyaev-Zel'dovich (ESZ) catalog revealed a significant population of massive clusters missed by optical/IR surveys like the Palomar Distant Cluster Survey (PDCS), challenging the completeness of galaxy-overdensity-based selection. Conversely, some PDCS clusters may be chance projections of unvirialized galaxy groups, not true massive halos. This directly impacts cosmological studies and cluster astrophysics, which rely on mass-complete samples.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant fraction of PDCS clusters, selected via optical/IR galaxy overdensities, are not massive, virialized dark matter halos, and that their optical richness is a biased and incomplete proxy for halo mass.
proposals.method: We will cross-match the PDCS catalog with archival X-ray data (e.g., eROSITA, XMM-Newton) to measure intracluster medium luminosity and temperature as direct mass proxies. We will also obtain dynamical mass estimates via spectroscopic follow-up of member galaxies using SDSS-V BHM and eFEDS ancillary programs. We will then compare these direct mass estimates to the PDCS richness measures, quantifying scatter, bias, and the fraction of PDCS candidates that are either low-mass systems or chance projections.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, multi-wavelength calibration of the PDCS sample's mass fidelity, quantifying the limitations of optical/IR selection. It will produce a cleaned, mass-verified cluster catalog for precise cosmological tests and improve the interpretation of future optical/IR surveys like LSST.
proposals.novelty: While individual PDCS clusters have been studied, a systematic, multi-probe mass calibration of the entire PDCS sample against X-ray and dynamical data has not been performed, leveraging the new synergy between SDSS-V spectroscopy and wide-area X-ray surveys like eROSITA.

proposals.title: The Evolution of Galaxy Cluster Abundance at 0.6 < z < 1.2 from the eROSITA Final Equatorial Depth Survey and SDSS-V Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: The abundance of galaxy clusters as a function of mass and redshift is a sensitive probe of cosmology, particularly the amplitude of matter fluctuations (σ₈) and dark energy. Pre-2026, robust constraints at z > 0.6 are limited, especially from wide-area X-ray surveys with extensive spectroscopic follow-up. The eROSITA Final Equatorial Depth Survey (eFEDS) provides a premier, uniformly selected X-ray cluster sample, while SDSS-V's Black Hole Mapper and Local Volume Mapper offer the multi-object spectroscopic capability to confirm members and measure precise cluster redshifts.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed evolution of the X-ray cluster mass function in the eFEDS field at 0.6 < z < 1.2 is consistent with a ΛCDM cosmology with parameters from Planck CMB data, but may reveal tension indicative of evolving dark energy or modified gravity.
proposals.method: We will construct a statistically complete sample of X-ray clusters from the eFEDS catalog. Using confirmed spectroscopic redshifts from SDSS-V (primarily from ancillary programs and the Black Hole Mapper's ability to target cluster galaxies) and legacy SDSS data, we will measure the cluster redshift distribution. We will derive cluster masses via established X-ray luminosity-to-mass scaling relations, calibrated with pre-2026 studies. The binned cluster number density as a function of redshift and mass will be compared to theoretical predictions from cosmological simulations using a likelihood analysis to constrain cosmological parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce one of the tightest constraints on cluster abundance evolution at z > 0.6 from a contiguous X-ray survey, directly testing the ΛCDM paradigm. It will demonstrate the synergistic power of eROSITA and SDSS-V for cosmology and provide a critical legacy sample for future studies of cluster astrophysics and galaxy evolution in dense environments.
proposals.novelty: While cluster cosmology is well-established, this proposal uniquely leverages the specific, contemporaneous combination of the eFEDS X-ray selection and the dedicated, wide-field spectroscopic follow-up capacity of SDSS-V, which was just beginning operations pre-2026, to push constraints to higher redshift with reduced systematic uncertainties from photometric redshifts.

proposals.title: A Matched Filter Census of Cosmic Voids in SDSS-V: Connecting Underdensities to AGN and Galaxy Properties
proposals.motivation: Cosmic voids are crucial probes of cosmology and large-scale structure formation, but their detection is often method-dependent and optimized separately from overdensity finders. The matched filter technique, used extensively to find clusters and groups in surveys like SDSS and eROSITA, provides a statistically robust framework for identifying significant density fluctuations. Applying this same, well-calibrated filter to systematically find underdensities offers a complementary, symmetric, and self-consistent map of the cosmic web.
proposals.hypothesis: The matched filter algorithm, when inverted to detect significant negative density contrasts, will efficiently identify a complete, well-defined sample of cosmic voids whose properties (size, density profile, redshift distribution) are directly comparable to the cluster sample found with the same technique.
proposals.method: We will adapt the established 3D spherical matched filter algorithm, used for cluster detection in SDSS and eROSITA data (e.g., eFEDS), to identify significant underdensities in the SDSS-V spectroscopic galaxy catalog. The filter will be convolved with the galaxy density field, derived from BOSS and future SDSS-V BHM data, to find peaks in the negative signal-to-noise ratio map. We will characterize void candidates by their core radius, density contrast, and significance, and cross-match their locations with the positions of AGN and galaxies from the SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper to study environmental influences.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first major void catalog from SDSS-V using a method directly analogous to its cluster-finding pipelines, enabling direct cosmological tests via the void size function and matter density profiles. It will also provide a unique environmental parameter—proximity to a void core—for statistical studies of AGN activity and galaxy evolution, testing if void galaxies host systematically different AGN populations.
proposals.novelty: While matched filters are standard for finding overdensities, their systematic application to find a cosmologically significant void sample within the same survey using the identical filter kernel is novel. This dual-use approach ensures a consistent statistical definition for both extremes of the density field.

proposals.title: Constraining Obscured AGN Populations through Spectral Decomposition of the Unresolved Cosmic X-ray Background with eROSITA and SDSS-V
proposals.motivation: A significant fraction (~20-30%) of the Cosmic X-ray Background (CXB) remains unresolved by deep Chandra and XMM-Newton surveys, representing a missing population of heavily obscured Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN). The spectral shape of this unresolved component is poorly constrained, limiting our understanding of the column density distribution and Compton-thick fraction of AGN across cosmic time. This gap directly impacts models of black hole growth and galaxy co-evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: The unresolved CXB emission is dominated by a population of heavily obscured (NH > 10^23 cm^-2) and Compton-thick AGN, and its spectral shape, when modeled with physically motivated AGN templates, will reveal a higher fraction of such objects than currently inferred from resolved-source studies.
proposals.method: We will perform detailed spectral modeling of the unresolved emission in the eROSITA Final Equatorial Depth Survey (eFEDS) field, leveraging its soft-to-hard band coverage. Using XSPEC, we will fit the unresolved CXB spectrum with a combination of absorbed AGN templates (e.g., MYTorus, BNTorus) and other known contributors (galaxies, clusters). Crucially, we will incorporate prior constraints from the co-located SDSS-V spectroscopic survey to fix the redshift distribution and intrinsic luminosity function of the AGN population, breaking degeneracies between obscuration and other spectral parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the most robust measurement to date of the spectral signature of the missing AGN population in the CXB, directly quantifying the contribution of heavily obscured and Compton-thick sources. It will deliver critical observational constraints for population synthesis models of black hole accretion and inform the design of future high-energy missions aimed at resolving this population.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of SDSS-V's optical spectroscopic priors on AGN demographics with eROSITA's wide-area X-ray spectral data for CXB analysis provides a powerful new methodology to deconvolve the unresolved emission, moving beyond simple phenomenological fits to a physically grounded population model.

proposals.title: A Local Laboratory: Multi-Wavelength Spectral Decomposition of Compton-Thick AGN in the eROSITA/SDSS-V Era
proposals.motivation: Compton-thick (CT) active galactic nuclei (AGN) are a crucial, yet poorly understood, component of the accretion history of the universe and the black hole-galaxy connection. While large surveys like SDSS and eROSITA identify statistical samples of obscured AGN, the detailed physics of the obscuring material and its connection to host galaxy properties remain ambiguous for the most heavily obscured systems. Studying nearby, well-resolved CT AGN provides an essential laboratory to dissect these processes at high spatial and spectral resolution, offering direct analogs for interpreting the integrated light of distant, unresolved populations.
proposals.hypothesis: Detailed multi-wavelength analysis of a carefully selected sample of local CT AGN will reveal systematic correlations between the properties of the obscuring torus (e.g., column density, covering factor), the ionized gas outflows, and the stellar populations of the host galaxy, which are not accessible in large, shallow surveys.
proposals.method: We will construct a volume-limited sample of ~20 confirmed local (z < 0.05) CT AGN from the overlap of the eROSITA eFEDS/eRASS and SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper surveys. For each target, we will perform a rigorous, multi-component spectral decomposition of SDSS-V optical spectra using the `pPXF` and `GANDALF` algorithms to separate the AGN continuum, broad/narrow emission lines, and host galaxy stellar absorption features. This will be combined with archival X-ray (Chandra, XMM-Newton) spectral fitting to constrain the obscuring column and intrinsic luminosity, and high-resolution HST or ground-based AO imaging to analyze host galaxy morphology.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a benchmark library of high-fidelity, multi-wavelength spectral models for local CT AGN, quantifying the dilution of AGN signatures by host galaxy light. It will directly test the efficacy of SDSS-V's Index Diagram and other diagnostic tools for identifying heavily obscured AGN in large surveys. The results will provide empirical calibration for models of AGN obscuration and feedback used in cosmological simulations.
proposals.novelty: While large surveys statistically characterize AGN populations, this proposal focuses on a deep, multi-wavelength *case study* approach for the most obscured local AGN, leveraging the synergy of eROSITA's X-ray selection with SDSS-V's optical spectroscopic power to move beyond demographic studies to detailed physical dissection.

proposals.title: Unveiling Hidden Hosts: A Systematic Search for Faint Extended Emission Around SDSS and eROSITA Point-Source AGN
proposals.motivation: Optical and X-ray surveys like SDSS and eROSITA primarily catalog point-source AGN, but their host galaxies, especially at high redshift or in a low-surface-brightness state, may be missed. This observational bias limits our understanding of the AGN-galaxy connection, particularly for low-luminosity or heavily obscured systems where the host's properties are crucial for contextualizing black hole growth.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant subset of AGN cataloged as point sources in SDSS and eROSITA possess previously undetected, faint extended emission from their host galaxies or associated large-scale nebular structures.
proposals.method: We will perform a systematic re-analysis of SDSS optical imaging for a large, well-defined sample of X-ray selected point sources from the eFEDS survey and optically selected SDSS quasars. Using custom PSF-subtraction techniques and low-surface-brightness optimized image stacking (e.g., median stacking in bins of redshift, luminosity, and X-ray hardness), we will search for residual extended flux. The analysis will leverage the deep, multi-epoch SDSS imaging and apply rigorous masking of contaminants to reach surface brightness limits fainter than standard pipeline processing.
proposals.contribution: This work will quantify the prevalence of 'hidden' hosts around point-source AGN, providing a more complete census of the AGN population. It will directly test for systematic biases in host galaxy studies and inform models of AGN fueling and feedback by revealing the true galactic environments of these compact cores.
proposals.novelty: While host galaxy studies exist for bright AGN, this proposal systematically targets sources previously classified as point-like, applying rigorous low-surface-brightness techniques from galaxy evolution studies to the AGN population in a large, statistical sample.

proposals.title: A Systematic Search for X-ray Dark AGN: Uncovering Obscured, Intrinsically Weak, or Radiatively Inefficient Quasars in eROSITA and SDSS-V
proposals.motivation: The established correlation between optical and X-ray luminosity in active galactic nuclei (AGN) underpins our understanding of accretion physics and AGN demographics. However, significant outliers to this relation, such as X-ray weak quasars or heavily obscured AGN, are critical for testing unified models and understanding the full AGN population. Pre-2026, systematic searches for such objects in wide, deep, and matched optical/X-ray surveys have been limited, leaving the nature and prevalence of X-ray dark but optically bright sources poorly constrained.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a population of AGN exists which are optically luminous (R<23) but undetected in deep X-ray surveys like eROSITA, due to factors including extreme obscuration, intrinsically weak coronal emission, or radiatively inefficient accretion flows.
proposals.method: We will cross-match deep optical sources (R<23) from the SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper and legacy SDSS imaging with the eROSITA Final Equatorial Depth Survey (eFEDS) and other public deep X-ray catalogs. For optical sources without X-ray counterparts, we will calculate sensitive X-ray upper limits at their optical positions using eROSITA exposure maps and background estimates. We will then construct the distribution of optical-to-X-ray flux ratios (α_OX) for these upper limits, identifying significant outliers (≥3σ) below the established α_OX-luminosity relation. Follow-up optical spectroscopy from SDSS-V will be used to classify source types, measure redshifts, and search for signatures of obscuration (e.g., high Balmer decrements, narrow lines only).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first statistically robust sample of X-ray dark, optically bright AGN from matched eROSITA/SDSS-V data, quantifying their space density. It will directly test models of AGN obscuration and corona formation, and identify prime candidates for follow-up multi-wavelength studies (e.g., with JWST or ALMA) to determine the physical origin of their X-ray weakness.
proposals.novelty: While individual X-ray weak quasars are known, a systematic search leveraging the specific combination of SDSS-V's spectroscopic completeness and eROSITA's uniform, deep X-ray coverage over a wide area is novel. This approach moves beyond serendipitous discovery to population-level analysis.

proposals.title: Simultaneous Bayesian Inference of Galactic Assembly and Stellar Orbits from Phase-Space Substructure
proposals.motivation: The Milky Way's assembly history is encoded in the phase-space structure of its stellar halo, including streams and substructures. Current methods typically assume a static or simple time-evolving potential to derive orbits, or assume an accretion history to model substructure, creating a circular problem. A unified framework is needed to co-infer the Galaxy's gravitational growth and stellar trajectories directly from stellar archaeology data, breaking this degeneracy.
proposals.hypothesis: A flexible, parameterized model of the Milky Way's time-dependent potential, directly linked to its mass accretion history, can be constrained simultaneously with the orbital histories of halo stars by fitting to the present-day positions, velocities, chemical abundances, and ages of stellar streams and substructures.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian hierarchical model. The time-dependent potential will be parameterized by the accretion history of massive satellites, using prescriptions from cosmological simulations (e.g., EMERGE). Orbits will be integrated within this evolving potential. The likelihood will compare model predictions to multi-survey data (SDSS, Gaia, DESI) for stream morphologies, stellar ages (from spectra), and phase-space overdensities. Inference will be performed via Hamiltonian Monte Carlo and nested sampling, yielding joint posteriors for accretion history and orbital ensembles.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first self-consistent, uncertainty-quantified reconstruction of the Milky Way's mass growth timeline directly from stellar kinematics and abundances. It will deliver a robust dynamical map of halo substructures and establish a new methodology to combine Galactic archaeology with galaxy formation models.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond using an assumed potential to derive orbits or an assumed history to fit substructure; instead, it performs a joint inference, treating both the Galactic potential's evolution and stellar trajectories as unknown quantities to be solved for simultaneously.

proposals.title: A Generative, Stochastic Model for Galactic Chemical Evolution: Testing Predictions Against SDSS-V APOGEE Abundance Distributions
proposals.motivation: The observed distribution of stellar abundances, particularly the [α/Fe] vs. [Fe/H] plane, encodes the Milky Way's assembly history. Pre-2026 interpretations often fit simple analytical tracks or identify discrete sequences, but these may not capture the emergent, stochastic nature of hierarchical growth from cosmological initial conditions. A first-principles generative model is needed to predict the full correlation structure of abundances and kinematics, providing a physical framework for interpreting large spectroscopic surveys.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed distribution of stellar α-abundances and their correlations with age and kinematics are an emergent outcome of the stochastic, hierarchical assembly of the Galactic disk from distinct star-forming regions with unique infall and enrichment histories.
proposals.method: We will develop a high-resolution, multi-zone chemical evolution model where the disk is an ensemble of independently evolving regions. Stochastic gas infall and radial migration will be governed by prescriptions from cosmological simulations (e.g., IllustrisTNG). Star formation and enrichment will follow resolved gas physics. The model will be implemented as a generative adversarial network (GAN) framework: a generator producing synthetic stellar populations from stochastic assembly histories, and a discriminator trained to distinguish these from real SDSS-V APOGEE DR17 data. The trained generator's latent space will map directly to physical assembly parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-principles, generative model of Galactic chemical evolution that predicts not just median trends but the full observed distribution and correlation matrix of abundances. It will provide a physical basis for interpreting substructure in abundance space and directly link statistical features in the data to parameters of Galactic assembly, moving beyond phenomenological fits.
proposals.novelty: The approach is generative and stochastic from first principles, rather than fitting smooth analytical tracks to data. It uses an adversarial framework to directly match the full, multi-dimensional distribution of observables, a method not previously applied to Galactic chemical evolution modeling.

proposals.title: Characterizing the High-Alpha Gap: A Spectroscopic Analysis of Stellar Populations in the SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper Survey
proposals.motivation: The gap in the [α/Fe] vs. [Fe/H] diagram for Milky Way stars is a key but poorly understood feature, potentially marking a hiatus in star formation or a distinct accretion event in the Galaxy's chemical evolution. Pre-2026, large spectroscopic surveys like SDSS have mapped this structure, but its precise boundaries, stellar population content (ages, kinematics), and physical origin remain ambiguous. Quantifying this gap is crucial for constraining models of Galactic assembly and enrichment history.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the high-alpha gap corresponds to a distinct, kinematically coherent stellar population with a narrow range of ages and chemical abundances, indicative of a specific accretion event or quenching episode in the early Galaxy.
proposals.method: We will leverage the SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper (BHM) spectroscopic survey, applying the Index Diagram methodology from recent work to cleanly separate quasar emission from host galaxy stellar continuum. For a large sample of gap and non-gap stars from BHM and legacy SDSS/APOGEE data, we will perform precise stellar parameter and abundance determinations using established pipelines (e.g., FERRE, The Cannon). We will then apply clustering algorithms (e.g., Gaussian Mixture Models, DBSCAN) in the multi-dimensional space of [α/Fe], [Fe/H], age proxies, and orbital actions to statistically define the gap's membership and properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first statistically robust definition of the high-alpha gap's stellar population, quantifying its chemical, age, and kinematic signatures. It will directly test competing formation scenarios (e.g., accretion vs. quenching) and provide critical empirical constraints for next-generation models of the Galactic thick disk and halo formation.
proposals.novelty: While the gap's existence is known, this proposal uniquely combines the SDSS-V BHM's powerful host-galaxy decomposition with advanced chemical cartography and multi-dimensional clustering to move from a descriptive diagram feature to a quantitatively defined astrophysical population.

proposals.title: The Origin of the Local Metal-Poor Tail: A Chemodynamical Analysis of SDSS-V and eROSITA Cross-Matched Sources
proposals.motivation: The local stellar metallicity distribution function (MDF) exhibits a tail of stars with [Fe/H] ~ -1, whose origin is unclear. Pre-2026 studies suggest contributions from accreted dwarf galaxies or in-situ formation in the early, turbulent Milky Way. Resolving this is key to understanding the Galaxy's earliest assembly and star formation, leveraging new large-scale spectroscopic and X-ray surveys.
proposals.hypothesis: The metal-poor tail ([Fe/H] < -0.8) in the local MDF is primarily composed of stars accreted from a small number of low-mass, ancient satellite galaxies, identifiable via distinct chemodynamical clustering.
proposals.method: We will construct a clean local sample of metal-poor stars using SDSS-V (Black Hole Mapper and Local Volume Mapper) optical spectra, deriving precise metallicities ([Fe/H]) and radial velocities. We will cross-match with eROSITA (eFEDS) X-ray sources to identify coronal activity, helping to vet the sample and probe stellar activity-metallicity links. Chemodynamical models (e.g., phase-space clustering analysis and orbit integration using Gaia EDR3 astrometry) will be applied to separate accreted populations from in-situ stars. The analysis will use established algorithms like HDBSCAN for dynamical clustering and mock data tests from cosmological simulations (e.g., FIRE-2) to interpret the results.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative assessment of the accretion history responsible for the local metal-poor tail, directly linking Galactic archaeology to merger tree predictions. It will demonstrate a novel application of SDSS-V/eROSITA synergy for stellar astrophysics and establish a framework for using combined multi-wavelength survey data to dissect Galactic components.
proposals.novelty: While metal-poor stars have been studied in halo surveys, a focused chemodynamical model of the *local* volume's metal-poor tail, combined with an X-ray/optical diagnostic to inform stellar selection, is a novel approach. This leverages the researcher's expertise in SDSS and X-ray source spectroscopy in a new stellar context.

proposals.title: The Radial Gradient of Galactic α-enhancement: A Systematic SDSS-V/APOGEE Census of High-[α/Fe] Stars in the Outer Halo
proposals.motivation: The [α/Fe] ratio is a key chemical clock tracing the star formation history of Galactic components. Pre-2026 studies using SDSS/APOGEE have mapped the Milky Way's inner regions, revealing a clear dichotomy between the high-α thick disk and low-α thin disk. However, the prevalence and distribution of high-α stars in the outer Galactic halo and its substructures remain poorly quantified, limiting our understanding of the early, rapid star formation episodes that built the Galaxy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-[α/Fe] ([α/Fe] > +0.2 dex) stars, signatures of rapid, early enrichment, are extremely rare in the outer Galactic zones (R_GC > 15 kpc, |Z| > 5 kpc) compared to the inner Galaxy, and that their spatial and kinematic distribution can isolate the earliest building blocks of the halo.
proposals.method: We will leverage the SDSS-V's Black Hole Mapper and Milky Way Mapper programs, combined with earlier APOGEE data, to assemble a large, homogeneous sample of stars with high-S/N H-band spectra. We will derive precise stellar parameters (Teff, log g) and abundances ([α/Fe], [Fe/H]) using the APOGEE Stellar Parameters and Chemical Abundances Pipeline (ASPCAP). Cross-matching with Gaia EDR3 astrometry will provide accurate distances and orbital parameters. We will then compute the volume-corrected number density of high-α stars as a function of Galactocentric radius and orbital eccentricity, statistically accounting for selection functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic, quantitative map of the Galactic α-enhancement gradient into the distant halo. It will directly constrain the radial extent of the Galaxy's early, high-star-formation-rate phase and identify chemically primitive stellar streams. The resulting catalog will serve as a benchmark for models of Galactic assembly and chemical evolution.
proposals.novelty: While APOGEE has characterized abundances in the inner Galaxy, a targeted, statistical analysis of the *rarity* of high-α stars in the outer halo, using the latest SDSS-V data to mitigate selection biases and extend coverage, is a novel and crucial test of inside-out formation scenarios.

proposals.title: Constraining Galactic Chemical Evolution with APOGEE: A Direct Confrontation of Abundance Gradients with Cosmological Simulations
proposals.motivation: The APOGEE survey has provided a revolutionary, homogeneous dataset of stellar elemental abundances across the Milky Way, enabling precise measurements of radial and vertical metallicity gradients. These gradients are a critical fossil record of the Galaxy's assembly and enrichment history. However, a comprehensive, quantitative comparison of these observed gradients with the predictions of state-of-the-art cosmological hydrodynamical simulations and detailed chemical evolution models remains a significant gap, limiting our ability to constrain key physical processes like gas accretion, radial flows, and star formation efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the detailed radial and [α/Fe] versus [Fe/H] gradients from APOGEE DR17, when analyzed in a spatially resolved manner, will rule out a significant fraction of existing cosmological galaxy formation models, revealing specific deficiencies in their implementations of chemical enrichment and radial migration.
proposals.method: We will compile a clean sample of red giant branch stars from APOGEE DR17, leveraging its precise radial velocities, stellar parameters, and abundances. We will derive robust radial abundance gradients for key elements (Fe, α, C, N) and construct [α/Fe] distribution functions in discrete Galactocentric bins. These measurements will be directly compared to mock stellar catalogs generated from cosmological simulations (e.g., TNG50, VINTERGATAN) and analytical chemical evolution models. The comparison will use a Bayesian framework to statistically assess model likelihoods, focusing on the shape, scatter, and normalization of the gradients as discriminants.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first statistically rigorous, multi-element test of cosmological simulations against the Milky Way's detailed chemical structure. It will identify which physical ingredients are necessary for models to reproduce the observed gradients, directly informing theories of galactic archaeology. The resulting public model-observation comparison toolkit will be a valuable resource for the community.
proposals.novelty: While gradients have been measured, a systematic, multi-model confrontation using the full statistical power of APOGEE's final data release and a Bayesian framework to discriminate between competing physical prescriptions has not been performed. This moves beyond qualitative comparison to quantitative model falsification.

proposals.title: Dynamical Tagging of Galactic Building Blocks: Phase-Space Identification of High-Alpha Accretion Events in the Halo with SDSS-V
proposals.motivation: The origin of the Milky Way's high-[α/Fe] halo stars remains a key question in Galactic archaeology. While the inner high-alpha population is often attributed to in-situ formation, the presence of such stars in the outer halo challenges this picture, suggesting they could be debris from ancient, massive satellite mergers. Pre-2026 large surveys like SDSS-V and Gaia provide the necessary 6D phase-space coordinates and precise abundances to test this accreted origin hypothesis directly.
proposals.hypothesis: High-[α/Fe] stars in the outer Galactic halo are not a monolithic, in-situ population but consist of distinct, dynamically coherent substructures that are the debris of specific, early accretion events.
proposals.method: We will construct a clean sample of high-[α/Fe] stars from SDSS-V's Black Hole Mapper and APOGEE surveys, cross-matched with Gaia astrometry for full 3D positions and velocities. For these stars, we will compute integrals of motion (energy, angular momentum). We will then apply unsupervised clustering algorithms (e.g., HDBSCAN) specifically within this high-alpha sub-sample in the space of these dynamical parameters to identify statistically significant, chemically homogeneous groups. The dynamical coherence and orbital properties of these groups will be analyzed to link them to proposed merger events like Gaia-Sausage-Enceladus.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a dynamical 'family tree' for the ancient, high-alpha halo, directly testing the accretion scenario. It will map specific phase-space clusters to probable progenitor systems, quantifying the contribution of early mergers to the chemically defined thick disk and inner halo. The methodology fuses chemical selection with dynamical tagging in a novel way to deconvolve Galactic assembly history.
proposals.novelty: Previous dynamical tagging studies typically analyze broad halo samples; this proposal uniquely applies the technique *after* a strict chemical selection (high-[α/Fe]), targeting the specific population whose origin is most debated. This fusion of a precise chemical cut with phase-space clustering is a new pathway to identify the accreted cores of ancient building blocks.

proposals.title: Testing the Representativeness of the Lockman Hole: A Multi-Wavelength Comparison of X-ray AGN Populations with SDSS-V and eROSITA
proposals.motivation: The Lockman Hole, a region of extremely low Galactic neutral hydrogen column density, has been a premier deep-field for X-ray and multi-wavelength surveys. It is often assumed to be representative of the general extragalactic sky. However, this assumption remains largely untested, and if false, it could bias our understanding of AGN demographics, evolution, and luminosity functions derived from deep-field studies. With the advent of wide-area surveys like eROSITA and SDSS-V, we can now perform a rigorous, statistical test of this foundational assumption.
proposals.hypothesis: The hypothesis to be challenged is that the X-ray selected AGN population (including spectral properties, redshift distribution, and host galaxy characteristics) in the Lockman Hole is statistically indistinguishable from populations in other, higher Galactic absorption areas of the sky at the same X-ray flux limits.
proposals.method: We will construct carefully matched samples of X-ray sources from the eROSITA Final Equatorial Depth Survey (eFEDS) and other eROSITA sky areas with varying Galactic N_H. For each source, we will utilize SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper spectroscopy to obtain redshifts, classify AGN type (e.g., using emission line diagnostics and broad absorption line identification), and measure key spectral features (e.g., using the Index Diagram method to separate AGN and host galaxy light). We will then perform rigorous statistical comparisons (e.g., Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests, multi-dimensional population modeling) of the redshift distributions, optical spectral properties, and AGN sub-type fractions between the Lockman Hole sample and control fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative assessment of the Lockman Hole's representativeness, which is critical for interpreting past and future deep-field observations. It will deliver a publicly available comparative catalog of X-ray AGN properties across different Galactic sightlines. The results will either validate the use of the Lockman Hole as a cosmic laboratory or identify specific population biases that must be accounted for in AGN evolutionary models.
proposals.novelty: While the Lockman Hole has been studied in depth, a direct, large-sample, spectroscopic comparison of its AGN population to other sky areas using modern, homogeneous surveys (eROSITA and SDSS-V) has not been conducted. This proposal leverages the unique combination of wide-area X-ray coverage and systematic optical spectroscopy from SDSS-V to test a long-standing, implicit assumption in extragalactic astronomy.

proposals.title: Constraining the Obscured AGN Population: A Spectroscopic Validation of Photometric Redshifts for eROSITA/SDSS Unidentified Sources
proposals.motivation: Large-area X-ray surveys like eROSITA are revealing a significant population of obscured Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN), crucial for understanding black hole growth and cosmic feedback. However, a substantial fraction of these sources lack secure spectroscopic redshifts, relying instead on photometric estimates which are notoriously uncertain for AGN with strong host galaxy contamination or heavy obscuration. Validating these redshifts is essential for determining accurate luminosities, space densities, and physical properties of the obscured AGN population.
proposals.hypothesis: Photometric redshifts for X-ray-selected, optically obscured AGN candidates, derived from multi-wavelength survey data (e.g., Legacy Survey, WISE), can be calibrated and validated to sufficient accuracy (Δz/(1+z) < 0.1) using targeted, deeper optical spectroscopy, enabling reliable demographic studies of this population.
proposals.method: We will select a sample of several hundred obscured AGN candidates from the eROSITA eFEDS and wider area surveys that are detected in SDSS photometry but lack secure SDSS spectroscopic redshifts. We will obtain deeper, targeted optical spectroscopy using the Baryon Oscillation Spectroscopic Survey (BOSS) spectrograph on the SDSS 2.5-m telescope, focusing on sources with high-quality photometric redshift PDFs. We will measure spectroscopic redshifts by fitting host galaxy stellar absorption features (e.g., Ca H+K, G-band, Mg b) and any weak, narrow emission lines. The performance of photometric redshift algorithms (e.g., using template fitting or machine learning like Random Forests) will be rigorously assessed against this new spectroscopic benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available catalog of validated redshifts for a key sample of obscured AGN, directly improving the reliability of subsequent luminosity function and clustering measurements. It will quantify the systematic uncertainties in AGN photometric redshifts and provide an empirical calibration to improve algorithms for future large-scale surveys like LSST. The results will strengthen the connection between X-ray selection and the optically identified AGN population.
proposals.novelty: While photometric redshifts are routinely tested for normal galaxies and unobscured quasars, this focused validation for X-ray-selected, optically obscured AGN—where the SED is a complex mix of AGN and host galaxy light—is not yet systematic. Leveraging the unique combination of eROSITA's X-ray selection and SDSS-V's spectroscopic capability targets a critical gap in demographic completeness.

proposals.title: The Co-evolution of AGN and Galaxy Groups in the UDS: A Joint X-ray and Optical Analysis of the Intra-Cluster Medium and Member Spectra
proposals.motivation: Galaxy groups and low-mass clusters are the most common galactic environments, yet their detailed baryonic physics, particularly the interplay between the intra-group medium (IGM) and active galactic nuclei (AGN) feedback, remains poorly constrained. The UKIDSS Ultra Deep Survey (UDS) field, with its unparalleled multi-wavelength coverage, provides a unique laboratory to study these systems at moderate redshifts. A systematic study leveraging both X-ray data (to trace the hot IGM and AGN) and deep optical spectroscopy (to characterize member galaxies and AGN) is critical for understanding group assembly and the impact of environment on black hole growth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the thermodynamic properties of the intra-group medium, as measured from X-ray observations, are directly correlated with the prevalence and accretion states of AGN within group member galaxies, as revealed by optical emission-line diagnostics. Specifically, we expect groups with disturbed or low-entropy IGM to host a higher fraction of optically-selected AGN, indicative of feedback or triggering mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will construct a robust sample of X-ray detected galaxy groups and clusters in the UDS field by cross-matching existing and upcoming X-ray source catalogs (e.g., from XMM-Newton, Chandra, and eROSITA) with optical/infrared group catalogs from the UDS. For each system, we will perform X-ray spectral fitting to derive IGM properties (temperature, density, metallicity, and luminosity). In parallel, we will utilize optical spectra from SDSS-IV/eBOSS and future SDSS-V data, applying the BHM 'Index Diagram' method to disentangle AGN emission from host galaxy light for all spectroscopically confirmed members. We will then perform a statistical analysis to correlate IGM properties with AGN fraction, luminosity, and accretion mode.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce one of the first detailed census of the AGN population across a well-defined sample of X-ray groups in the UDS, directly linking large-scale environmental conditions to nuclear activity. It will constrain the role of AGN feedback in group environments and provide empirical scaling relations for the IGM properties of groups at z~0.5-1.0, a key epoch of cluster assembly.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined X-ray groups or AGN in clusters separately, this proposal uniquely combines detailed X-ray IGM characterization with the sophisticated optical spectral decomposition techniques of SDSS-V's Black Hole Mapper to isolate the AGN signal within the same group sample, enabling a direct, multi-phase test of AGN-environment co-evolution.

proposals.title: The Intrinsic Structure of AGN Outflows: High-Resolution Spectroscopy of Representative X-ray Selected AGNs from eROSITA and SDSS
proposals.motivation: Large-scale surveys like SDSS and eROSITA have statistically characterized AGN populations, but their typical spectral resolution (R~2000) blends key diagnostic emission and absorption lines, obscuring the detailed kinematics and ionization structure of outflows. Understanding these outflows is crucial for quantifying AGN feedback, yet the physical conditions in the broad-line region and inner absorbers of 'typical', non-extreme AGNs remain poorly constrained due to a lack of high-resolution data for representative samples.
proposals.hypothesis: High-resolution (R>10,000) optical spectroscopy of a carefully selected, representative sample of X-ray detected AGNs will reveal previously unresolved kinematic substructure (e.g., multiple velocity components, non-gaussian wings) in emission lines like C IV and [O III], and will detect weak, narrow absorption features that are blended in SDSS spectra, providing a more accurate picture of typical outflow properties.
proposals.method: We will select ~30 representative AGNs from the eFEDS/SDSS spectroscopic catalog, spanning a range of luminosities and redshifts (z~0.5-2.5), ensuring they are not known extreme BAL quasars. We will obtain high-resolution (R~20,000) spectra using the Keck HIRES or VLT UVES spectrographs. Data reduction will use standard pipelines (e.g., MAKEE, ESOREX). Analysis will employ spectral decomposition techniques (e.g., multi-component Gaussian fitting in IRAF or custom Python routines) to isolate the AGN continuum, broad emission lines, and narrow absorption features, measuring precise velocities, widths, and asymmetries.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic, high-resolution spectral atlas of 'normal' X-ray selected AGNs, directly measuring the distribution of outflow velocities and densities in the representative population. It will establish empirical constraints for models of wind launching and provide benchmark data to calibrate lower-resolution spectral diagnostics from SDSS-V and future massive surveys.
proposals.novelty: While high-resolution studies exist for peculiar AGN subtypes, this focused investigation of a representative, X-ray selected sample bridges the gap between population statistics and detailed physics, moving beyond the characterization of outliers to define the intrinsic properties of the general AGN population.

proposals.title: The Obscured Extragalactic Sky: A Multi-Wavelength Survey of a High Galactic Absorption Field with eROSITA and SDSS-V
proposals.motivation: Ultra-deep surveys in minimal Galactic absorption fields, like the Lockman Hole, have been foundational for studying the cosmic X-ray background and AGN evolution. However, this creates a selection bias, as a significant fraction of the extragalactic sky lies behind substantial Galactic neutral hydrogen (NH) and dust. The properties of AGN and galaxies in these high-absorption zones remain poorly characterized, potentially missing a population of sources whose optical/UV emission is significantly extinguished, or introducing systematic errors in large-area statistical studies that avoid these regions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep, multi-wavelength survey in a carefully selected region of high Galactic absorption will reveal a population of AGN with optical spectroscopic properties (e.g., emission line ratios, host galaxy contamination) systematically different from those in clean fields, due to the compounding effects of Galactic and intrinsic extinction.
proposals.method: We will select a ~25 deg² field with Galactic NH > 5x10²¹ cm⁻², utilizing the eROSITA Final Equatorial Depth Survey (eFEDS) X-ray source catalog as the primary input. Optical spectroscopy for all X-ray sources will be obtained using the SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper (BHM) fiber spectrographs. We will employ the BHM 'Index Diagram' methodology—measuring spectral indices in specific bands around emission lines and the continuum—to disentangle AGN power from host galaxy light and quantify reddening. Redshifts will be determined via cross-correlation and emission line fitting. X-ray hardness ratios from eROSITA will be used to assess intrinsic obscuration and correlate with optical spectral properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first statistically complete census of AGN behind high Galactic NH, quantifying the impact of foreground extinction on AGN detection and classification. It will test the completeness of all-sky AGN surveys and provide an empirical correction for source counts. The combined eROSITA/SDSS-V dataset will serve as a critical benchmark for planning observations with future infrared facilities like JWST and Roman in obscured sky regions.
proposals.novelty: While many surveys avoid high-absorption zones, this proposal deliberately targets such a region as a strategic 'dual' to the Lockman Hole, leveraging the novel synergy between eROSITA's soft X-ray sensitivity and SDSS-V's spectroscopic capability to systematically probe the obscured extragalactic sky for the first time.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Spectroscopic Incompleteness on AGN Evolution Models from Multi-Wavelength Surveys
proposals.motivation: Large surveys like SDSS and eROSITA rely on spectroscopic redshifts to measure AGN luminosity functions and test cosmic evolution models. A fundamental, often implicit, assumption is that the spectroscopically confirmed sample is a complete and unbiased representation of the parent photometric or X-ray selected population. This assumption is rarely tested, and systematic incompleteness could bias key evolutionary parameters like density or luminosity evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spectroscopic incompleteness in multi-wavelength AGN surveys is non-uniform and depends on parameters like flux, color, and source morphology, leading to significant biases in derived luminosity functions and evolution models when using standard apportioned number count techniques.
proposals.method: We will leverage the investigator's expertise with SDSS and eROSITA cross-matched samples (e.g., eFEDS). Using the parent X-ray/optical photometric catalog, we will apply machine learning techniques (e.g., Random Forest classifiers) trained on known spectroscopic redshifts to model the probability of redshift success as a function of observables. We will then implement a Bayesian hierarchical framework to forward-model the observed spectroscopic sample, incorporating these completeness weights to infer the true underlying AGN luminosity function and its evolution, marginalizing over the incompleteness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, quantitative framework to correct for spectroscopic selection effects in AGN surveys. It will deliver revised constraints on AGN evolution parameters from SDSS/eROSITA data and create a public tool for assessing completeness biases in future SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper studies.
proposals.novelty: While incompleteness is acknowledged, most studies treat it simplistically. This proposal inverts the standard assumption by making incompleteness the central, quantitatively modeled variable in the inference process, directly integrating it into the evolution model fitting rather than treating it as a secondary correction.

proposals.title: Deblending Confused eROSITA X-ray Sources with Bayesian Methods: A New Tool for the Black Hole Mapper
proposals.motivation: The eROSITA all-sky survey and deeper pointed surveys like eFEDS are detecting vast numbers of faint X-ray sources, many of which are AGN. At the faint flux limit, source confusion—where multiple astrophysical objects lie within the X-ray point-spread function—becomes a significant contaminant, biasing source catalogs and complicating multi-wavelength identification. This is a critical problem for SDSS-V's Black Hole Mapper, which relies on clean samples of X-ray-selected AGN for spectroscopic follow-up to study accretion physics and demographics.
proposals.hypothesis: Bayesian deblending techniques, proven in deep radio continuum surveys (e.g., DEEP2, ASKAP), can be successfully adapted to model and separate confused X-ray sources in eROSITA data, significantly improving source purity and enabling the recovery of true, faint source fluxes and positions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the probabilistic framework of the Bayesian deblending algorithm used in the LOFAR Two-metre Sky Survey (LoTSS), which models the sky as a collection of point and Gaussian sources with priors on flux and position. This will be applied to eROSITA cutouts, using the known X-ray point-spread function as the kernel. Priors will be informed by high-resolution multi-wavelength data (e.g., optical positions from SDSS, near-infrared from unWISE) to constrain the number and locations of potential counterparts. The posterior distributions for flux and position of each blended component will be sampled using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, publicly available software tool for X-ray source deblending and a refined catalog of deconfused faint eROSITA sources. It will directly benefit SDSS-V's Black Hole Mapper by providing more reliable input source lists for spectroscopic targeting, leading to cleaner samples of AGN for studies of emission line properties and accretion states. The method will also enhance the scientific yield of eROSITA catalogs for population studies.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian deblending is standard in radio astronomy, its application to X-ray survey data is novel. This approach moves beyond simple positional cross-matching or aperture photometry, providing a statistically rigorous, multi-wavelength-informed solution to the X-ray confusion problem that has not been systematically implemented for eROSITA.

proposals.title: Astrometric Deblending of Faint eROSITA/SDSS-V Sources: A Census of Obscured AGN and Starburst Galaxy Demographics
proposals.motivation: Wide-field surveys like eROSITA and the SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper are revealing vast populations of faint, X-ray-emitting sources. At these flux limits, source confusion and large positional uncertainties from standard centroiding techniques prevent reliable optical counterpart identification and classification. This ambiguity obscures the demographics of key populations, such as low-luminosity AGN and distant starburst galaxies, which are critical for testing models of black hole growth and galaxy evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: Advanced astrometric forward modeling, applied to the optical imaging of X-ray source error regions, can statistically deblend confused sources and separate the distinct spatial distributions of AGN-dominated hosts from those of pure starburst galaxies, enabling a reliable demographic census below the nominal photometric detection limit.
proposals.method: We will develop a forward-modeling pipeline that uses probabilistic cataloging (e.g., the Bayesian framework of PCAT) on deep optical (SDSS, DESI Legacy Imaging Surveys) cutouts at the positions of faint eROSITA sources from the eFEDS and subsequent SDSS-V BHM fields. The method will simultaneously fit for the positions, fluxes, and simple morphologies of all potential sources within the X-ray error circle, using the X-ray-to-optical prior and the distinct light profile of a point-like AGN versus an extended starburst galaxy. This will be coupled with SDSS-V optical spectroscopy to validate the classifications for a bright subset and inform the priors for the faint population.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first statistically robust separation of AGN and star-forming galaxy populations in the faint, confused regime of current wide-field surveys. It will deliver new constraints on the faint end of the X-ray luminosity function for obscured AGN and the star-forming galaxy main sequence at moderate redshifts, directly testing galaxy evolution and AGN feedback models.
proposals.novelty: This approach inverts the standard assumption that photometric detection must precede classification; instead, we use astrometric and morphological precision from probabilistic modeling to perform demographic science *below* the traditional detection limit, a novel synergy of X-ray survey data with advanced optical image analysis.

proposals.title: Constraining the AGN Host Galaxy Population at the eROSITA/SDSS-V Flux Limit: A Hierarchical Bayesian Inversion of Confused Spectra
proposals.motivation: In X-ray surveys like eROSITA, source populations near the flux detection threshold are statistically confused, with contributions from both normal and starburst galaxy AGN. Pre-2026 analyses often assume a dominant host type to interpret stacked or population properties, but this assumption remains untested at the faintest detectable fluxes, directly impacting our understanding of AGN demography and accretion physics.
proposals.hypothesis: The intrinsic mix of AGN host galaxy types (normal vs. starburst) can be robustly inferred from the joint distribution of noisy optical spectra and X-ray fluxes for sources at the eROSITA detection threshold, without assuming a dominant population.
proposals.method: We will apply a hierarchical Bayesian population model to a sample of X-ray sources from the eFEDS/SDSS-V overlap with spectroscopic redshifts and optical spectra. The model will treat each source's intrinsic X-ray flux and host galaxy type (parameterized via spectral indices from the SDSS-V BHM 'Index Diagram') as latent variables. Using forward modeling, we will incorporate the eROSITA flux likelihood, SDSS spectral noise, and the explicit X-ray selection function. Inference via Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling will recover the intrinsic joint distribution of host type and flux, correcting for Eddington bias and measurement error.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first statistically rigorous measurement of the AGN host galaxy population mix at the eROSITA confusion limit, moving beyond assumption-based stacking. It will deliver a public framework for population inversion in thresholded surveys and directly inform physical interpretations of the faint X-ray source counts from eROSITA and future missions.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard stacking analyses that assume a single dominant population, this method explicitly models and infers the mix of host types from confused data. It leverages the new SDSS-V spectral diagnostics and Bayesian hierarchical techniques to turn the 'barely-observed' population into a quantifiable constraint.

proposals.title: Unifying the AGN Zoo: A Deep Field Monitor Survey to Link Transient Discovery with Physical Drivers via Embedded Ultra-Deep Puncta
proposals.motivation: Current time-domain surveys (e.g., ZTF, ASAS-SN) excel at discovering variable AGN and transients but lack the deep, continuous monitoring needed to probe underlying accretion physics. Conversely, deep, pointed X-ray/optical monitoring (e.g., Chandra Deep Fields) provides exquisite detail but over tiny areas, missing the connection to the wider population of rare, bright events. A hybrid survey is needed to bridge the discovery space of wide-field programs with the diagnostic power of deep fields.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed diversity of AGN variability and transient phenomena (e.g., changing-look events, tidal disruption flares) can be unified by physical processes that are revealed only through the combination of a wide-area census and ultra-deep, continuous light curves of a strategically selected anchor population.
proposals.method: We propose a 2 Ms X-ray/optical survey (leveraging eROSITA, Chandra, and SDSS-V optical spectroscopy) with a wide, shallow mosaic (80% time) and embedded ultra-deep puncta (20% time on 5-10 pre-selected fields). The wide component will identify rare, bright variables. For objects in the puncta and a matched sample from the wide field, we will construct unprecedented, high signal-to-noise light curves.
