We will apply the Index Diagram method (from recent SDSS-V work) to puncta spectra to disentangle host galaxy contamination and measure virial BH masses, correlating these physical parameters with variability properties measured from the deep light curves.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first catalog directly linking the detailed physics of a deep, well-characterized AGN sample (from puncta) with the behavior of a large, volume-complete sample of rare transients. It will establish whether changing-look AGN and extreme variability events have distinct physical drivers or exist on a continuum with normal AGN variability, fundamentally advancing our understanding of accretion disk instability and obscuration models.
proposals.novelty: The strategic fusion of wide-area discovery and embedded ultra-deep monitoring within a single, coherent survey design is novel. It moves beyond the trade-off between area and depth by using the puncta as physical anchors to interpret the wide-field transients, a methodology not yet implemented in major legacy surveys.

proposals.title: Calibrating the Cosmic Web: A Multi-Tiered X-ray/Optical Survey for AGN Clustering and Photometric Redshift Systematics
proposals.motivation: Large-scale structure (LSS) studies using AGN as tracers are limited by the tension between survey volume and source density. Wide, shallow surveys find rare objects but lack the depth for robust clustering measurements, while deep, narrow surveys sample dense structure but suffer from cosmic variance. Furthermore, precise clustering requires accurate redshifts, but spectroscopic follow-up for millions of X-ray sources from surveys like eROSITA is infeasible, making photometric redshifts (photo-z) critical yet a major source of systematic error.
proposals.hypothesis: A strategically stratified survey design, combining wide-area discovery with nested deep-field calibration, can simultaneously enable the discovery of rare AGN populations and deliver precise LSS measurements by using the deepest data to train and correct photo-z systematics in the wider fields.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-tiered 'wedding cake' survey using 2 Ms of X-ray (e.g., Chandra) exposure, partitioned into wide (shallow), medium, and deep components. SDSS-V optical spectroscopy, leveraging the Black Hole Mapper, will target sources in all tiers. We will apply the Index Diagram method to deblend AGN and host galaxy light for pure spectral templates. These templates will train a photometric redshift pipeline (e.g., using a Random Forest or neural network architecture like a simple multilayer perceptron) on the deep, spectroscopically complete tier. The calibrated pipeline will then be applied to the wider tiers to correct systematic photo-z errors before performing clustering analyses (e.g., two-point correlation functions).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a unified catalog of X-ray AGN with robust photo-z's, enabling the first precise clustering measurements of AGN across a wide dynamic range in luminosity and redshift. It will directly quantify and mitigate photo-z systematics for AGN, a crucial step for next-generation cosmology surveys. The stratified design also optimizes the discovery of rare populations like high-luminosity or peculiar BAL quasars.
proposals.novelty: While tiered surveys exist, the explicit integration of a deep, spectroscopic calibration tier—using AGN-specific spectral decomposition from SDSS-V—to correct systematics in the photo-z's of a wider X-ray selected AGN sample for LSS analysis is a novel, self-calibrating approach not yet implemented in pre-2026 AGN cosmology studies.

proposals.title: The SDSS-V/eROSITA Wide-Area Shallow Mosaic: A Census of High-Luminosity AGN and Large-Scale Structure
proposals.motivation: Deep, pencil-beam surveys like the Chandra Deep Fields are biased against rare, high-luminosity quasars and large-scale structures due to their limited volume. A comprehensive census of the most luminous active galactic nuclei (AGN) and robust measurements of cosmic filaments require contiguous, wide-area data with uniform spectroscopic coverage, a gap in pre-2026 multi-wavelength surveys.
proposals.hypothesis: A wide-area, shallow X-ray/optical mosaic with a total exposure equivalent to a 2 Ms deep field will discover a statistically significant population of rare, high-luminosity AGN and enable precise clustering measurements of AGN and galaxies on scales >100 Mpc, inaccessible to deep fields.
proposals.method: We propose a dedicated SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper and eROSITA collaboration to create a 1000 deg^2 contiguous mosaic with a uniform ~2 ks eROSITA X-ray exposure, matched by SDSS-V optical spectroscopy. AGN will be identified via X-ray/optical matching and spectral line diagnostics (e.g., BPT diagrams, broad emission lines). Large-scale structure will be mapped using the redshift-space two-point correlation function and filament-finding algorithms (e.g., DisPerSE) on the complete spectroscopic sample.
proposals.contribution: This will yield the first volume-limited sample of high-luminosity (L_X > 10^45 erg/s) AGN at z~1-3, constraining the bright end of the AGN luminosity function. It will also produce definitive measurements of AGN clustering and baryon acoustic oscillations on the largest scales, providing a critical test for cosmological models and galaxy formation scenarios.
proposals.novelty: This strategically reallocates a traditional deep-field time investment to a wide, shallow design, explicitly prioritizing volume and contiguity over depth to solve distinct astrophysical problems that deep fields cannot address.

proposals.title: An Iterative Bayesian Framework for Astrometric Refinement and Optical Property Prediction of eROSITA X-ray Sources with SDSS-V
proposals.motivation: The accurate cross-identification of X-ray sources (e.g., from eROSITA/eFEDS) with optical counterparts (e.g., from SDSS-V) is fundamental for multi-wavelength studies of AGN and galaxies. Current methods often rely on fixed positional error radii, leading to ambiguous matches or missed identifications for faint or blended sources. A method that dynamically refines X-ray positions using source-specific priors could significantly improve match reliability and purity, especially for large surveys.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an iterative Bayesian framework, which uses optical properties predicted from X-ray data to inform astrometric priors, can produce more accurate X-ray source positions and more reliable optical identifications than standard fixed-error matching, creating a self-improving cycle for source characterization.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage model. First, a Random Forest or simple neural network, trained on securely matched eFEDS-SDSS-V sources, will predict optical magnitude (r-band) and broad source class (AGN, galaxy, star) from X-ray flux, hardness ratio, and extent. Second, these predictions will inform a Bayesian cross-matching algorithm (e.g., a modified NWAY) where the prior positional uncertainty is a function of predicted optical brightness and class. The improved matches will then be used to retrain the property predictor, creating an iterative loop. We will validate against deep spectroscopic identifications from SDSS-V BHM.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, publicly available tool for statistically robust X-ray/optical matching with quantified improvements over standard likelihood ratio techniques. It will produce a refined catalog of eFEDS sources with higher-confidence optical counterparts and predicted properties, directly benefiting SDSS-V's Black Hole Mapper and future multi-wavelength studies. The iterative framework demonstrates a novel synergy between astrometry and spectral energy distribution modeling.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop feedback between predictive photometric modeling and astrometric refinement, moving beyond static error circles to use the anticipated nature of the optical counterpart itself to guide the positional solution, a concept not yet widely implemented in large survey pipelines pre-2026.

proposals.title: The L-T Transition in X-ray Selected Brown Dwarfs: A Multi-wavelength Census with SDSS-V and eROSITA
proposals.motivation: The L-T spectral type transition in brown dwarfs marks a critical astrophysical phase where atmospheric dust settles and methane forms, dramatically altering spectral energy distributions. However, the physical drivers of this transition—particularly the roles of luminosity, temperature, and potential accretion or activity—remain poorly constrained observationally. Pre-2026, large, multi-wavelength samples targeting this specific transition are rare, limiting our understanding of substellar evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that objects at the L-T transition identified via optical/infrared spectroscopy will exhibit a distinct, correlated range of X-ray and UV properties traceable to changing atmospheric physics and magnetic activity, and that these properties will differ systematically from those of earlier- and later-type brown dwarfs.
proposals.method: We will construct a definitive sample by cross-matching L and T dwarfs from SDSS-V (BOSS/APOGEE) optical/IR spectra with X-ray counterparts from the eROSITA Final Equatorial Depth Survey (eFEDS) and archival Chandra/XMM-Newton data. We will measure precise spectral types, luminosities (L_bol), and effective temperatures from the SDSS spectra using template fitting and spectral indices (e.g., the H2O-K index). For matched sources, we will derive X-ray luminosities (L_X) and hardness ratios, and search for UV counterparts in GALEX data to constrain chromospheric activity. Statistical comparisons will be made to control samples of earlier (M/L) and later (T/Y) dwarfs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first statistically significant catalog of X-ray properties for brown dwarfs spanning the L-T transition, directly testing models of atmospheric cloud evolution and magnetic dynamo operation in ultracool atmospheres. It will establish empirical scaling relations between L_X/L_bol, spectral type, and temperature, providing critical benchmarks for theoretical models of substellar evolution and planet atmospheres.
proposals.novelty: While SDSS and eROSITA have been used to study AGN and stars, their combined power has not been systematically applied to the specific problem of the L-T transition in brown dwarfs. Leveraging the SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper's spectral analysis tools (like the Index Diagram) for these cool objects, in tandem with sensitive X-ray data, is a novel, multi-messenger approach to a long-standing stellar/substellar problem.

proposals.title: Unveiling the Obscured Population: A Search for AGN with No Optical Counterparts in eROSITA and SRG/eROSITA Hard X-ray Catalogs
proposals.motivation: The census of active galactic nuclei (AGN) is heavily biased toward sources with clear optical signatures, such as broad emission lines. Heavily obscured AGN, particularly those with Compton-thick absorption, may lack these signatures, leading to their under-representation in optical surveys like SDSS. Identifying these objects is crucial for understanding the true demographics of supermassive black hole growth and the cosmic X-ray background.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant population of heavily obscured AGN exists that are detected in hard X-rays (e.g., 2-10 keV) but lack optical spectroscopic signatures of AGN activity, either due to extreme obscuration or host galaxy dilution.
proposals.method: We will cross-match the SRG/eROSITA all-sky survey hard X-ray source catalog (pre-2026 data releases) with optical photometric and spectroscopic archives, primarily SDSS (DR17) and the Legacy Surveys. For X-ray sources without a secure optical counterpart or with only a host galaxy spectrum, we will perform forced spectroscopic follow-up using the SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper (BHM) fiber system. We will analyze these spectra using the Index Diagram method, developed in recent SDSS-V work, to disentangle AGN emission from stellar light and quantify obscuration via X-ray-to-optical flux ratios and spectral hardness.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly quantify the fraction of hard X-ray-selected AGN missing from optical surveys, providing a key constraint on the obscured AGN population. It will produce a well-defined sample of optically elusive AGN for detailed multi-wavelength study, testing models of torus geometry and accretion physics in extreme environments.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have matched X-ray and optical catalogs, this proposal systematically targets the *lack* of an optical AGN signature as the primary selection criterion, leveraging the new, large-area hard X-ray catalogs from eROSITA and the dedicated follow-up capacity of SDSS-V BHM to probe this population efficiently.

proposals.title: Unveiling the Disk-Wind Connection: High-Resolution Time-Resolved Spectroscopy of Bright AGN Flares
proposals.motivation: Statistical studies of large AGN samples, like those from SDSS and eROSITA, have established broad correlations between X-ray properties, broad emission lines, and outflow signatures like BALs. However, these ensemble averages obscure the physical processes driving rapid accretion and feedback. The dynamics of the accretion disk, corona, and wind are encoded in spectral variability on timescales of hours to days, which remains poorly resolved for individual sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that coordinated, high-cadence spectroscopic monitoring of the brightest AGN during optical/UV flares will reveal correlated changes in broad emission line profiles and continuum components, directly probing the causal link between accretion disk instabilities and the launching/ionization state of outflows.
proposals.method: We will select the 5-10 brightest, most variable AGN from the SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper and eFEDS catalogs. Using the Hobby-Eberly Telescope (HET) with its High-Resolution Spectrograph (HRS), we will obtain optical spectra (R~30,000) at a cadence of ~1-2 days over multiple 30-night semesters, triggered by enhanced activity from ASAS-SN or ZTF. We will apply the Index Diagram method (from recent SDSS-V work) to decompose host galaxy light and isolate the pure quasar spectrum. Time-series analysis of specific emission lines (e.g., C IV, Hβ) will track changes in velocity, flux, and asymmetry.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, dynamical maps of the broad-line region response to continuum flares in individual AGN, moving beyond reverberation mapping's luminosity-weighted averages. It will directly test models of disk-wind geometry and provide empirical constraints on the timescale for wind ionization state changes, a key uncertainty in feedback models.
proposals.novelty: While large surveys excel at population studies, this proposal shifts focus to intensive, high-resolution study of single objects in a time domain rarely accessed for spectroscopy. It uniquely combines the source selection and spectral decomposition tools of SDSS-V with the temporal resolution needed to dissect flare physics.

proposals.title: Constraining the Unresolved Cosmic X-ray Background with SDSS-V/eROSITA Cross-Matched AGN Catalogs
proposals.motivation: The cosmic X-ray background (CXB) is dominated by emission from active galactic nuclei (AGN), but its exact composition, particularly the contribution of unresolved point sources versus truly diffuse emission, remains uncertain. This limits our understanding of the total accretion history of the universe and the nature of the faintest AGN populations. The advent of deep, wide-area surveys like eROSITA and the spectroscopic follow-up capabilities of SDSS-V provide an unprecedented opportunity to address this.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by constructing a complete luminosity function and spectral energy distribution model for X-ray-selected AGN with SDSS-V spectroscopy, we can accurately predict and subtract their integrated flux, revealing a residual CXB spectrum that is significantly harder and fainter than current estimates, constraining the population of heavily obscured or low-luminosity sources.
proposals.method: We will leverage the eFEDS and future eROSITA All-Sky Survey data matched to SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper spectroscopic catalogs to create a flux-limited sample of resolved AGN with known redshifts, luminosities, and optical spectral properties (e.g., emission line diagnostics). Using X-ray spectral fitting and absorption corrections, we will model the intrinsic SED of each source. We will then integrate the emission from this population over redshift and luminosity, using the observed X-ray luminosity function, to create a model of the resolved CXB. This model will be subtracted from the total observed CXB spectrum from eROSITA and other missions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the most robust measurement to date of the truly unresolved CXB, setting stringent upper limits on the contribution from any missing populations. It will directly inform models of supermassive black hole growth and provide critical constraints for the interpretation of future high-energy observatories. The methodology will also deliver a refined, spectroscopically validated AGN luminosity function from the combined SDSS-V/eROSITA dataset.
proposals.novelty: While population synthesis models of the CXB exist, this approach uniquely leverages the large, *spectroscopically confirmed* and *optically characterized* AGN sample from SDSS-V to anchor the model in directly observed source properties, minimizing assumptions about completeness and obscuration corrections inherent in purely photometric or X-ray-only studies.

proposals.title: Disentangling Environmental and Redshift Effects on AGN Activity: A Controlled Study of SDSS-V and eROSITA Cross-Matched Samples
proposals.motivation: Observations from SDSS and X-ray surveys like eROSITA have reported an enhancement of AGN activity within large-scale structures (LSS). However, it remains unclear if this is a true environmental effect driven by local galaxy density or a spurious correlation induced by redshift-dependent selection biases in flux-limited surveys, which can conflate luminosity, redshift, and density. Resolving this ambiguity is critical for understanding the role of environment in triggering and fueling supermassive black holes.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed correlation between AGN activity and environmental density is primarily a selection effect arising from the redshift-dependent sensitivity of optical and X-ray surveys, rather than a physical consequence of enhanced triggering in overdense regions.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled sample of AGN from the SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper and the eFEDS/eROSITA All-Sky Survey, leveraging precise spectroscopic redshifts and X-ray properties. Using volume-limited sub-samples and mock catalogs from cosmological simulations (e.g., IllustrisTNG), we will apply identical selection functions to simulated AGN to predict observed density contrasts. We will then compare the observed AGN density field, measured via correlation functions and group/cluster catalogs, against these predictions, explicitly testing for residual environmental enhancement after accounting for selection effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, quantitative assessment of the true environmental dependence of AGN activity, free from major selection biases. It will deliver a definitive test for models of AGN triggering in dense environments and establish a methodology for future studies with the full SDSS-V and eROSITA datasets.
proposals.novelty: While AGN-environment studies exist, this proposal uniquely combines the latest SDSS-V spectroscopy with wide-area X-ray data from eROSITA and employs a forward-modeling approach with simulations to explicitly isolate and remove redshift-dependent selection effects, moving beyond simple correlation analyses.

proposals.title: Merging Histories of AGN Hosts: A Direct Test of the Merger-Driven Evolution Scenario with SDSS-V and eROSITA
proposals.motivation: The galaxy merger scenario is a leading theoretical framework for explaining AGN triggering and the co-evolution of supermassive black holes and their hosts. However, observational evidence connecting AGN activity directly to merger signatures across cosmic time remains ambiguous and often contradictory. A robust, statistically significant test using large, multi-wavelength samples is needed to clarify the role of mergers in driving AGN host galaxy evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: If galaxy mergers are the primary driver of AGN host evolution, then AGN hosts at a given redshift and luminosity will exhibit a significantly higher incidence of morphological disturbances and close-pair companions compared to a carefully matched sample of non-AGN galaxies.
proposals.method: We will construct a large, multi-wavelength sample of AGN by cross-matching SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper optical spectra (providing emission line diagnostics and redshifts) with X-ray sources from eROSITA (e.g., eFEDS), ensuring robust AGN identification. For each AGN host, we will use high-resolution optical imaging from DESI Legacy Surveys and, where available, HSC or HST, to quantify non-parametric morphological indicators (e.g., Gini, M20, asymmetry) via established algorithms like statmorph. We will also identify close kinematic pairs using SDSS spectroscopic redshifts. A control sample of non-AGN galaxies will be matched in redshift, stellar mass, and environment. The merger fraction as a function of AGN luminosity, redshift, and Eddington ratio will be the key diagnostic.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the most direct and statistically powerful test to date of the merger-driven AGN host evolution scenario, quantifying the merger fraction across a broad range of AGN properties. It will establish whether mergers are a necessary or sufficient condition for AGN activity and constrain the evolutionary phase in which mergers most strongly influence AGN fueling.
proposals.novelty: Leveraging the new, systematically selected SDSS-V/eROSITA AGN sample moves beyond biased, single-waveband selections. The combined analysis of detailed host morphology and spectroscopic close pairs provides a more complete census of merger signatures than previous studies, which often relied on a single diagnostic.

proposals.title: The X-ray and Optical Properties of Low-Luminosity AGNs in the eFEDS Field: A Comparative Study with Quasars
proposals.motivation: Studies of X-ray selected AGNs, such as those from eROSITA's eFEDS survey, have historically focused on luminous quasars, leaving the population of low-luminosity AGNs (LLAGNs, log Lx < 41.9) less explored in a systematic, multi-wavelength context. Understanding these LLAGNs is crucial for probing the faint end of the AGN luminosity function, the connection between AGN activity and host galaxy evolution, and the potential differences in accretion physics. A direct, identical analysis of LLAGNs in the same field and redshift range as luminous quasars is needed to isolate luminosity-dependent effects from selection biases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that low-luminosity AGNs in the eFEDS field will exhibit systematically different X-ray-to-optical flux ratios and emission line properties compared to more luminous quasars, potentially indicating a higher incidence of host galaxy dilution, different accretion states, or increased obscuration.
proposals.method: We will select a sample of LLAGNs (log Lx < 41.9) from the eFEDS X-ray source catalog, matched to the same redshift range (e.g., z < 0.8) as the luminous quasar sample from recent SDSS-V/eFEDS work. Using SDSS-V optical spectroscopy from the Black Hole Mapper program, we will perform an identical spectral analysis pipeline, employing the Index Diagram method to disentangle AGN and host galaxy stellar contributions. We will measure key emission line properties (e.g., [O III], Hβ, Hα) and compute X-ray-to-optical flux ratios (α_ox), directly comparing these metrics to the published results for the higher-luminosity eFEDS quasars.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first uniform comparison of X-ray and optical spectral properties between LLAGNs and luminous quasars from the same survey field, establishing a robust baseline for the faint end of the AGN population. It will quantify the impact of host galaxy light on AGN diagnostics at low luminosities and test for luminosity-dependent trends in accretion efficiency or obscuration, informing models of AGN demography and feedback.
proposals.novelty: While LLAGNs have been studied, a direct, apples-to-apples comparison using the identical analysis pipeline and survey data (eFEDS/SDSS-V) as used for luminous quasars is novel. This approach uniquely controls for instrumental and methodological biases, allowing us to isolate the intrinsic effects of AGN luminosity.

proposals.title: Constraining Stellar Mass-to-Light Ratios in AGN Host Galaxies via Halo Mass Inversion from SDSS and eROSITA
proposals.motivation: In AGN studies, decomposing the host galaxy's stellar component from the nuclear emission is crucial for understanding black hole-galaxy co-evolution. Traditional methods fit stellar population models to the observed spectrum to estimate the stellar mass, but these are degenerate with the AGN continuum and sensitive to dust. An independent constraint on the total galactic mass, particularly the dark matter halo, could break this degeneracy and provide a novel check on stellar population synthesis models in these complex systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The dark matter halo mass of an AGN host galaxy, derived from large-scale environment measures or dynamical tracers, can be used to constrain the plausible range of its inner stellar mass, thereby inverting the standard modeling sequence to yield empirical constraints on the stellar mass-to-light ratio (M*/L).
proposals.method: We will leverage large, multi-wavelength samples from SDSS-V (optical spectra) and eROSITA (X-ray positions/AGN selection). For a sample of Type 1 AGN, we will derive virial halo masses using established techniques such as clustering measurements (cross-correlation with spectroscopic galaxies) or halo occupation distribution modeling from the SDSS legacy survey. We will then use the robust relationship between halo mass and total stellar mass established for inactive galaxies (pre-2026) to estimate the expected total stellar mass for each AGN host. Comparing this to the directly observed luminosity of the host component—decomposed using tools like the SDSS-V Index Diagram or spectral PCA—will yield a distribution of M*/L constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first population-level, empirically anchored estimates of M*/L in AGN host galaxies, independent of stellar population model assumptions. It will test for systematic differences in the stellar populations of AGN hosts compared to inactive galaxies and inform the calibration of host galaxy subtraction in large quasar surveys like the Black Hole Mapper.
proposals.novelty: Instead of using stellar mass to inform halo mass, we invert the approach, using the externally constrained halo mass as a prior to investigate the stellar component. This leverages the well-established halo-mass stellar-mass relation in a new way to solve a long-standing problem in AGN host galaxy characterization.

proposals.title: Constraining the Galactic Baryon Fraction: A Combined Analysis of SDSS-V BHM Spectra and eROSITA X-ray Halos
proposals.motivation: A long-standing problem in galaxy formation is the 'missing baryons' issue, where the observed baryon fraction within the virial radius of galaxies like the Milky Way falls significantly short of the cosmic fraction (Ω_b/Ω_m). Pre-2026 constraints rely on multi-wavelength tracers with significant systematic uncertainties in mass modeling and baryon census. The advent of SDSS-V's Black Hole Mapper (BHM) and eROSITA's all-sky survey provides a new, synergistic opportunity to refine these measurements.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying virial mass estimators calibrated on quasar broad-line regions to a large sample of Milky Way-analog galaxies, and combining this with X-ray-derived coronal gas masses from eROSITA, we can produce a more robust and systematically consistent estimate of the baryon fraction within R_vir, revealing it to be higher than previous estimates but still below the cosmic value.
proposals.method: We will construct a sample of ~10,000 low-redshift (z < 0.3) galaxies from SDSS-V BHM, selected to have stellar masses and velocity dispersions analogous to the Milky Way. For each, we will derive a central supermassive black hole mass (M_BH) using the Hβ or Mg II broad emission line virial relations, then infer the host dark matter halo mass (M_vir) using the locally calibrated M_BH-σ* and M_vir-σ* relations. We will cross-match this sample with the eROSITA Final Equatorial Depth Survey (eFEDS) and the all-sky survey to measure the soft X-ray emission from their hot circumgalactic halos, converting luminosity to hot gas mass via density and temperature profiles. The total baryonic mass (stars + cold gas + hot gas) will be compared to M_vir.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, emission-based estimate of the Galactic baryon fraction anchored in the well-understood physics of quasar broad-line regions. It will provide a critical, independent check on results from absorption-line studies and hydrodynamical simulations. The findings will directly inform models of baryonic feedback and accretion by quantifying the retained baryon budget in present-day galaxies.
proposals.novelty: The key innovation is using the quasar central engine, observed via SDSS-V BHM spectra, as a precise dynamical probe of the host galaxy's gravitational potential, bypassing traditional stellar kinematic modeling. Combining this with the first statistical X-ray halo mass measurements from eROSITA for a large, homogeneously selected galaxy sample creates a uniquely powerful methodology.

proposals.title: Constraining Dark Matter Profiles in Milky Way Satellites: A Mock-Observation Analysis of Stellar Kinematics
proposals.motivation: The inner density profiles of dwarf galaxies are a critical test of the Lambda-Cold Dark Matter (ΛCDM) model, with tensions like the 'cusp-core' problem persisting. Milky Way satellites provide the best-resolved kinematic data for such tests, but traditional Jeans modeling suffers from degeneracies between the stellar anisotropy and the dark matter potential. A robust, statistically rigorous method to compare theoretical predictions directly with observational data is needed to resolve these uncertainties.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a forward-modeling, mock-observation framework to the stellar kinematics of classical Milky Way satellites will robustly discriminate between cusped (NFW) and cored dark matter density profiles, revealing systematic trends with galaxy luminosity or dynamical mass.
proposals.method: We will adapt the mock-observation comparison method, successfully used for quasar spectral analysis in SDSS-V, to the dynamical modeling of dwarf spheroidals. For a given theoretical dark matter profile, we will generate a large suite of mock stellar populations with realistic positions, velocities, and measurement errors matching extant datasets (e.g., from Keck/DEIMOS). We will apply the same Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithms used in Jeans analysis to these mock datasets to recover the input parameters. The key step is comparing the posterior distributions from the real data directly to the distributions recovered from the mock data, quantifying biases and degeneracies inherent to the analysis method itself.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce robust, methodologically transparent constraints on the dark matter distributions in the best-studied dwarf galaxies. It will directly quantify the systematic uncertainties in profile measurements, moving the field beyond simple best-fit comparisons. The results will provide a crucial local anchor for galaxy formation models and dark matter physics.
proposals.novelty: While mock data tests are common, applying a full mock-observation pipeline—where the *entire analysis chain* (data reduction, measurement, modeling) is applied identically to simulations and real data—to dwarf galaxy kinematics is novel. This approach, inspired by techniques in large-scale AGN surveys, systematically isolates observational biases from astrophysical signals.

proposals.title: Linking AGN Host Halo Mass to Galactic Accretion Histories: A Predictive Framework for the Milky Way's Stellar Halo
proposals.motivation: The assembly history of the Milky Way's stellar halo, a fossil record of galactic cannibalism, is a key test of hierarchical galaxy formation models. While studies of halo substructure (e.g., Gaia-Enceladus) reveal past mergers, a predictive, quantitative link between a galaxy's present-day dark matter halo mass and its specific accretion history remains elusive. This gap limits our ability to interpret the Milky Way's history within the broader context of galaxy evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: The present-day virial mass of the Milky Way's dark matter halo, as inferred from the dynamics of its satellite population and AGN host scaling relations, encodes sufficient information to statistically predict the mass spectrum and accretion epochs of its major stellar halo progenitors.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS-V) Black Hole Mapper and eROSITA-selected AGN samples to calibrate robust halo mass estimators (e.g., using clustering, satellite kinematics, or halo occupation distribution models) for a large sample of Milky Way-analog galaxies (specific star formation rate, stellar mass). For these analogs, we will use deep optical spectra to measure stellar population gradients and chemical abundances in their outskirts, serving as proxies for accretion history. We will then employ a Gaussian Process regression framework, trained on cosmological hydrodynamical simulations (e.g., IllustrisTNG, EAGLE), to learn the mapping between present-day halo mass and the distribution of progenitor masses and merger redshifts, applying the resulting model to Milky Way mass constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirically-calibrated, quantitative model that uses a galaxy's halo mass to predict its stellar halo assembly history. It will provide a new interpretive framework for Milky Way archaeology, allowing specific halo substructures to be placed within a statistical context of expected mergers. Furthermore, it will establish a novel connection between AGN host demographics (via halo mass) and the merger-driven growth of galactic halos.
proposals.novelty: While halo mass is known to correlate with overall merger history, directly predicting the *stellar* halo accretion spectrum from present-day mass is a novel inversion problem. The approach uniquely combines SDSS-V/eROSITA AGN halo mass calibration, stellar population synthesis of galactic outskirts, and machine learning applied to cosmological simulations to build this predictive bridge.

proposals.title: Unveiling the Faint Extended Universe: A Systematic Re-analysis of SDSS-V/eROSITA Point Sources with Extended Emission Models
proposals.motivation: Modern wide-field surveys like SDSS and eROSITA rely heavily on point-source detection algorithms, which can misclassify marginally resolved, low-surface-brightness galaxies or galaxy groups as point sources. This biases demographic studies of active galactic nuclei (AGN) and their host galaxies, particularly in the faint regime where the contrast between nuclear and extended emission is low. Correcting this misclassification is crucial for understanding the true connection between supermassive black hole growth and galaxy evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant fraction of optically faint sources in SDSS-V/eROSITA cross-matched catalogs, currently classified as point-like, are in fact low-redshift, low-surface-brightness galaxies or galaxy groups whose extended emission has been missed due to sensitivity limits and modeling assumptions.
proposals.method: We will select a sample of optically faint (r > 21) sources from SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper and eFEDS cross-matches initially classified as point sources. Using SDSS optical imaging, we will perform detailed 2D surface brightness modeling with tools like GALFIT, employing both point-spread-function (PSF) and Sérsic/extended galaxy models. We will compare the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) of the fits to robustly identify objects where an extended model is statistically preferred. Spectroscopic redshifts from SDSS and morphological parameters from the fits will then be used to characterize the newly identified extended population.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a cleaned catalog of genuinely point-like AGN and a newly identified sample of faint extended galaxies, correcting a systematic bias in current samples. It will directly test the 'point source assumption' in large surveys and provide more accurate constraints on the faint end of the galaxy luminosity function and the AGN fraction in low-mass, diffuse systems.
proposals.novelty: While extended source analysis is common for bright objects, systematically applying it to *faint survey sources pre-classified as point-like* to uncover a hidden population is a novel, inverse approach. Leveraging the statistical power of SDSS-V's spectroscopic follow-up of eROSITA X-ray sources for this purpose is also unique.

proposals.title: A Systematic Search for AGN Variability and Transients in the 1 Ms eROSITA eFEDS Field: Correlating X-ray Light Curves with SDSS-V Optical Spectra
proposals.motivation: X-ray time variability is a powerful, yet under-explored, probe of accretion physics in Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) and can reveal transient phenomena like tidal disruption events (TDEs). The eROSITA Final Equatorial Depth Survey (eFEDS) provides a unique, wide-area dataset with deep, 1 Ms X-ray light curves, but a comprehensive variability analysis of its point sources, correlated with optical spectroscopic classifications from SDSS-V, has not been performed. This work will bridge the gap between X-ray timing properties and optical spectral features (e.g., emission lines, BALs) to understand the physical drivers of AGN variability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic analysis of eFEDS 1 Ms light curves will reveal distinct classes of variable sources, where the amplitude and timescale of X-ray variability correlate with optical spectral properties (e.g., Eddington ratio, BAL presence, host galaxy contamination) from SDSS-V, enabling the identification of rare, extreme variability events indicative of changing-look AGN or TDEs.
proposals.method: We will analyze the 1 Ms light curves for all point sources in the eFEDS field using established pre-2026 methods: calculating the normalized excess variance to quantify variability amplitude and applying the Lomb-Scargle periodogram to search for quasi-periodic oscillations. Sources will be cross-matched with the SDSS-V Black Hole Mapper spectroscopic catalog, leveraging the 'Index Diagram' method from recent work to disentangle AGN and host galaxy contributions. We will classify variable sources using a combination of variability parameters (e.g., fractional rms, characteristic timescale) and their optical spectral properties (e.g., line widths, redshifts, spectral indices).
proposals.contribution: This project will produce the first catalog of X-ray variable sources in the eFEDS field with robust optical spectroscopic classifications, quantifying the relationship between X-ray variability and AGN spectral type. It will identify candidate changing-look AGN and TDEs for follow-up, and provide empirical constraints on accretion disk models by linking variability to fundamental parameters like black hole mass and accretion rate derived from SDSS-V spectra.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have analyzed eFEDS source catalogs or SDSS-V spectra separately, the novelty lies in the direct correlation of *quantified X-ray light curve properties* from a long, sensitive exposure with the *detailed, spatially-resolved optical spectral diagnostics* now available from SDSS-V, creating a multi-epoch, multi-wavelength view of AGN physics not previously possible at this scale.

proposals.title: A Systematic Search for X-ray Transients in the eROSITA eFEDS Field: Adapting Optical Time-Domain Algorithms to a 1 Ms X-ray Timeline
proposals.motivation: X-ray variability is a powerful probe of accretion physics in AGN and stellar systems, but systematic searches for transient and variable X-ray sources have been limited by sparse temporal coverage. The eROSITA eFEDS survey provides a unique, contiguous 1 Ms X-ray timeline, offering an unprecedented opportunity to detect variability on timescales from hours to years. Applying mature algorithms from optical time-domain surveys to this dataset can systematically identify new classes of X-ray transients and characterize the variable X-ray sky.
proposals.hypothesis: Algorithms developed for optical transient detection (e.g., from ZTF or SDSS Stripe 82) can be successfully adapted to identify and classify variable and transient sources in the eFEDS 1 Ms light curves, revealing populations of extreme variability AGN (e.g., Changing-Look Quasars), tidal disruption events, and flaring stellar systems missed by traditional X-ray analyses.
proposals.method: We will adapt the architecture of the ZTF transient detection pipeline, which uses a reference image subtraction and source association framework, to work with eFEDS X-ray photon event lists and derived light curves. Key steps include: generating binned light curves for all eFEDS sources; implementing a robust change-point detection algorithm (like the Bayesian Blocks algorithm) to flag significant flux changes; and applying feature extraction (e.g., rise time, amplitude, symmetry) for machine learning classification (Random Forest/ XGBoost) using known variable templates from XMM-Newton and Chandra. The pipeline will be validated on known variable sources in the field.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic catalog of X-ray transients and variables from the eFEDS deep timeline, quantifying the incidence of extreme variability in the X-ray AGN population. It will establish a reusable pipeline for time-domain analysis of future eROSITA all-sky survey data and bridge methodologies between optical and X-ray time-domain astronomy.
proposals.novelty: While optical transient pipelines are mature, their direct application to a deep, contiguous X-ray timeline is novel. This approach moves beyond simple variability indices to implement full-fledged transient detection and classification, leveraging the specific cadence and sensitivity of the eFEDS observations.

ideas.title: Wide-Field Census for Rare and Large-Scale Phenomena
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from epidemiology, where wide, shallow population surveys are used to find rare conditions and map large-scale health trends. The analogous astronomical solution is to conduct a 'Wide-Field Census for Rare and Large-Scale Phenomena.' By distributing the same total 2 Ms exposure time over a much wider area in a shallow, systematic mosaic, the survey is optimized not for depth but for volume and contiguity. This strategy will efficiently discover rare, bright objects like high-luminosity quasars or rare transients that are missed in deep-but-narrow fields, while simultaneously providing the large-area, uniform data essential for robust statistical analysis of cosmic large-scale structure, filamentary networks, and cosmological parameters.

ideas.title: Dynamical Tagging of Ancient Accretion: Phase-Space Identification of High-Alpha Stars in the Galactic Halo
ideas.description: This research direction applies dynamical system analysis and phase-space clustering techniques specifically to the sub-population of high-[α/Fe] stars in the outer Galactic zones. By isolating stellar groups in integrals-of-motion space that are both dynamically coherent and chemically homogeneous (high-alpha), the method can identify distinct streams or clusters that are likely debris from specific, ancient accretion events that built the inner Galactic halo. This directly tests the hypothesis that outer halo high-alpha stars are primarily accreted rather than formed in situ, providing a dynamical 'family tree' for these rare stars. The result is a reconstructed map of the dynamical and chemical signatures of the Galaxy's earliest major mergers.

ideas.title: Stratified Wide Survey with In-Depth Calibration for Discovery and Cosmology
ideas.description: This redesigned approach strategically partitions the 2 Ms total exposure into a multi-tiered 'wedding cake' structure. A very wide, shallow component efficiently discovers rare, bright objects. Crucially, nested medium and deep tiers provide the galaxy density and depth needed for robust clustering measurements. The deepest tier, targeting a small area, generates a high-fidelity sample for training and calibrating photometric redshifts, which are then applied to correct systematic errors in the wider tiers. This integrated design overcomes the fatal flaw of the original by enabling both discovery science and precise large-scale structure analysis from the same observational investment.

ideas.title: The Deep Field Monitor: A Wide-Area Survey with Embedded Ultra-Deep Time-Domain Puncta
ideas.description: This approach distributes the 2 Ms total exposure time to create a wide, shallow mosaic survey, but strategically allocates a significant fraction of the time (e.g., 10-20%) to repeatedly revisit a small number of pre-selected, scientifically rich 'puncta' within that wide field at ultra-deep depth. The wide component discovers rare, bright variable and transient objects across a large cosmic volume. Crucially, the embedded ultra-deep puncta provide an unprecedented, continuous time-domain baseline within the same survey, enabling detailed study of source physics, precursor activity, and environmental correlations for objects found in the wide field, while also serving as the deepest ever monitoring of their own rare, faint variable populations.

ideas.title: Astrometrically-Informed Cross-Band Identification: Using Predicted Optical Properties to Refine X-ray Source Positions
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel iterative framework that combines the prediction of optical properties from X-ray data with advanced astrometric refinement techniques. First, a model predicts the likely optical brightness and source type (e.g., AGN, star, galaxy) from X-ray flux and color. These predictions then inform a sophisticated astrometric analysis, guiding the matching or centroiding algorithm with source-specific priors to potentially surpass the standard 0.3 arcsec positional uncertainty. The improved positions enable more accurate and confident cross-identifications with optical catalogs, which then provide higher-quality training data to iteratively refine the initial optical property prediction model, creating a synergistic feedback loop.

ideas.title: Astrometric Deblending for Faint Galaxy Demographics
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the assumption that matched-filter/centroiding provides optimal positions by developing and applying advanced astrometric techniques (e.g., forward modeling, probabilistic cataloging) specifically to push below the nominal detection limit of wide-field surveys. The goal is to invert the dominant source type assumption by using the improved positional and morphological information from these techniques to statistically separate and classify the populations of normal versus starburst galaxies in the faint, confused flux regime. This synergy enables a novel demographic census at fluxes where traditional photometric methods fail due to blending and large centroid errors, directly testing galaxy evolution models.

ideas.title: Chronometric Orbits: Co-inferring Stellar Trajectories and Galactic Assembly History
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework to compute stellar orbits and the Milky Way's growth history simultaneously, rather than assuming one to study the other. It employs a flexible, physically motivated model for the time-dependent gravitational potential, parameterized by its accretion history. The model is constrained by fitting not just to present-day stellar positions and velocities, but crucially to the morphologies and ages of stellar streams and the phase-space structure of halo substructures, which serve as integrated records of the time-varying potential. This Bayesian inversion yields a posterior distribution of both plausible Galactic growth histories and the corresponding orbital ensembles, providing robust, uncertainty-quantified insights into dynamical history.

ideas.title: Scale down: Monitor low-luminosity AGNs with same UV lines

ideas.title: Generative Model of Galactic Chemical Evolution from Stochastic Hierarchical Assembly
ideas.description: This approach uses high-resolution, multi-zone simulations that treat the Galactic disk as an ensemble of star-forming regions, each with a unique and stochastic infall and enrichment history dictated by cosmological accretion and radial migration. The model generates a predicted distribution of alpha-abundances as an emergent outcome from first principles of gas physics and star formation, not by fitting a continuum. Its key testable prediction is the specific correlation structure between alpha-elements and other observables (e.g., age, kinematics, other abundances) that should arise from this assembly process, providing a physical basis for any continuum or substructure found in the data.

ideas.title: Bayesian Population Inversion at the Flux Confusion Limit
ideas.description: This project directly tests the dominant galaxy type assumption by analyzing the statistically confused population near the detection threshold, not below it. It uses a hierarchical Bayesian framework to model the joint distribution of intrinsic flux and galaxy type (normal vs. starburst) from noisy, thresholded observational data. The model explicitly corrects for selection effects and measurement error, allowing robust inference of the intrinsic population mix down to the faintest reliably probed fluxes. This turns an untestable conjecture about the unobserved into a statistically rigorous measurement of the barely-observed, providing a concrete, falsifiable answer.

ideas.title: A Wide-Area, Multi-Depth Survey to Empirically Map the Breakdown of Poissonian Background Statistics
ideas.description: This fused idea proposes a tiered survey design that strategically distributes a fixed total exposure time (e.g., 2 Ms) across a wide field, creating a mosaic of pointings with depths ranging from very shallow to ultra-deep. This creates a controlled experiment where the same instrumental and astrophysical background components are sampled at vastly different exposure times across a single contiguous area. By analyzing the background statistics in each depth bin, the survey can empirically determine the exposure threshold at which Poisson assumptions break down and directly identify the astrophysical causes (e.g., faint source clustering, diffuse large-scale emission) responsible for the deviation. Simultaneously, the wide-area component detects the rare, bright objects and large-scale features whose correlated faint counterparts may be driving the non-Poissonian noise in the deepest regions.

ideas.title: Statistical Deconvolution of the Faint Galaxy Population
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from environmental DNA (eDNA) analysis, where the genetic material of many organisms is mixed in a single sample, to astronomy's challenge of characterizing populations below the detection limit. In eDNA, statistical deconvolution against reference genomes determines species abundance without direct isolation. Similarly, for confused astronomical fields at depths where individual galaxy detections fail, we propose using the integrated light in pixels or small regions as a composite signal. By applying Bayesian mixture models or stacking analyses with well-defined spectral templates for normal and starburst galaxies, we can statistically determine their relative contributions and luminosity functions at ultra-faint fluxes, directly testing which population truly dominates below the catalog limit.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Predict optical properties from X-ray data

ideas.title: Astrophysical Single-Cell Genomics: Ultra-deep, multi-messenger monitoring of a single variable source
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from single-cell genomics, where the full analytical depth is applied to one unique cell, to astrophysics. Instead of surveying many objects, the entire 2 Ms Chandra exposure, plus coordinated 'deep-field' time from other major observatories, is dedicated to a single, scientifically compelling variable source (e.g., a tidal disruption event, a highly variable quasar, or an extreme magnetar). The goal is to perform a holistic, time-domain, multi-messenger 'assay' of this single cosmic 'cell'. This would generate a complete, simultaneous picture of its behavior across all accessible physical channels (X-ray variability, spectral evolution, correlated optical/IR/radio flux changes, etc.), potentially revealing interconnected physical processes and rare states that are invisible in shallower studies of many objects or in uncoordinated observations.

ideas.title: Chandra Aspect Self-Calibration: A Rigorous Error Model for Field-Based Astrometric Refinement
ideas.description: This project develops a novel 'aspect self-calibration' technique specifically for Chandra X-ray Observatory astrometry. Instead of directly importing radio interferometry algorithms, it first derives a physically accurate error model from Chandra's aspect system and historical data. The core algorithm then iteratively solves for corrections to the spacecraft attitude parameters (roll, pitch, yaw) and optical model terms by minimizing the residuals between detected X-ray source positions and their known optical/infrared counterparts across the entire field of view. This approach directly addresses the dominant source of astrometric error, creating a robust, physics-based refinement system that can significantly improve absolute positioning for sources lacking bright counterparts.

ideas.title: Bayesian Dynamical Inference for Faint Multi-Wavelength Stellar Streams
ideas.description: This research direction develops a Bayesian hierarchical framework that fuses dynamical systems analysis with probabilistic source validation. The method treats the orbital actions of low-significance source candidates (e.g., from X-ray, UV, or infrared catalogs) as data, using a dynamical model for a stellar stream or cluster as the prior. It then performs joint inference to simultaneously assess the probability that a coherent dynamical structure exists and determine the probabilistic membership of each marginal detection, rigorously quantifying uncertainties. This enables the discovery and characterization of faint stellar streams by leveraging their expected phase-space coherence across the entire multi-wavelength dataset, significantly boosting detection confidence for structures invisible in any single survey.

ideas.title: Calibrate Galactic Chemical Evolution Models Using a Data Assimilation Framework
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to solve the problem of confronting measured abundance gradients with model predictions by adapting a solution from climate science. In that field, data assimilation techniques like Ensemble Kalman Filters are used to rigorously merge heterogeneous observational data with complex numerical models, adjusting parameters to produce an optimal, physically consistent analysis. Adapted to an astrophysical context, the idea is to develop a data assimilation framework for galactic chemical evolution. This framework would iteratively assimilate APOGEE's detailed abundance gradients for multiple elements into an ensemble of cosmological zoom-in simulations or parametric chemical evolution models. The process would not just perform a static comparison but would actively adjust model parameters (e.g., star formation laws, feedback efficiencies, radial gas flows) to identify the range of initial conditions and physical processes that can simultaneously reproduce the observed multi-element gradients, thereby providing constrained, probabilistic evolutionary histories for the Milky Way's disk.

ideas.title: A Poisson-Statistical Convolutional Network for Rigorous X-ray Source Detection and Characterization
ideas.description: This redesigned system uses a deep learning architecture whose first layer explicitly computes the Poisson log-likelihood ratio between source-present and background-only models for each image patch, embedding the statistical reality of the data. It is trained entirely on synthetic data generated from a detailed instrument simulator that includes accurate PSF, vignetting, and complex background components. The network outputs a detection significance map and simultaneous parameter estimates (position, extent, flux) with well-calibrated uncertainties. This approach maintains the pattern-recognition power of CNNs while providing statistically rigorous, quantifiable results that integrate seamlessly with traditional X-ray astronomy analysis pipelines.

ideas.title: Astrometric Precision Benchmark: A Hybrid Differentiable-Probabilistic Framework for Fundamental Limits
ideas.description: This research establishes a controlled benchmark to determine the fundamental astrometric limits for a well-defined observational scenario, moving beyond vague comparisons. It generates a high-fidelity synthetic dataset with known truth positions, simulating specific challenges like crowding, undersampling, and complex PSFs from instruments like Gaia or the Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope. The core methodological advance is a hybrid model combining a differentiable physics-based simulator of the optics and detector with a probabilistic neural network, trained to output a full posterior distribution for each source position. This framework allows for a statistically rigorous, blind-test evaluation against traditional centroiding and matched-filter techniques, quantifying any improvement and its dependence on source parameters and noise regimes.

ideas.title: Adapting Super-Resolution Microscopy's Point Spread Function Engineering to Astrometric Centroiding
ideas.description: This idea challenges the foundational assumption that matched-filter/centroiding based on the telescope's theoretical point spread function provides the optimal stellar positions, currently yielding a median 0.3 arcsec uncertainty. By drawing an analogy from super-resolution microscopy, where engineering and analyzing the Point Spread Function has broken the classical diffraction limit, we propose a novel astrometric approach. In microscopy, techniques like STED physically shrink the PSF, while PALM/STORM use computational single-molecule localization. Adapted to astrometry, this suggests investigating whether the effective PSF for positional measurement can be sharpened computationally using detailed detector and atmospheric models, or by employing statistical photon localization methods on dithered data. The goal is to determine if such 'PSF engineering' can yield astrometric precision substantially better than the 0.3 arcsec limit imposed by standard centroiding techniques.

ideas.title: Bayesian Disambiguation of Multi-Messenger Counterparts: Probabilistic Optical Predictions from X-ray Data and Context
ideas.description: This redesigned system tackles the inherent degeneracy in predicting optical properties from X-ray data alone. It employs a hierarchical Bayesian framework that integrates X-ray flux, color, and variability patterns with contextual priors (e.g., source location, catalog cross-matches) to model the posterior probability distribution over possible optical brightness, type, and source class. The output is not a single prediction but a set of probabilistically weighted solutions with associated confidence intervals, explicitly quantifying the ambiguity. This approach transforms a flawed direct mapping into a robust tool for prioritizing follow-up observations and identifying likely multi-wavelength counterparts in survey data.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Tomography Techniques to Map Galactic Abundance Structures
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard assumption of simple radial abundance gradients in galaxies by adapting a solution from geophysics. In seismology, tomography solves the inverse problem of mapping the Earth's complex internal structures from sparse surface measurements. Analogously, we propose treating a galaxy's stellar population as a network of discrete sensors. By using the precise chemical abundances and 6D positions (from surveys like Gaia, APOGEE, and GALAH) of millions of stars as input data, we can develop a computational inversion framework. This adapted 'galactic chemical tomography' would reconstruct the underlying continuous, non-axisymmetric abundance patterns—such as spiral arm enhancements, azimuthal variations, or mixing by bars—providing a dynamic, 3D view of the galaxy's chemical evolution and enrichment history.

ideas.title: Apply Bayesian False Discovery Rate control for low-significance X-ray source validation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Bayesian False Discovery Rate (FDR) control methodology, widely used in genomics to identify differentially expressed genes from noisy microarray data, to the astrophysical problem of validating low-significance X-ray source candidates. In genomics, the method models observed test statistics as a mixture from a null distribution (no biological effect) and an alternative distribution (real effect), using Bayesian inference to compute the posterior probability of a true discovery for each candidate. Adapted to X-ray astronomy, we would model the observed distribution of source detection significances (or related statistics) across a field or catalog as a mixture of background Poisson fluctuations and genuine, but faint, astrophysical sources. By incorporating prior knowledge about the expected logN-logS source count distribution, the point spread function, and the local background characteristics, a Bayesian hierarchical model can be constructed. This model would output, for each low-significance candidate, the posterior probability that it is a real astrophysical source rather than a background fluctuation. This provides a statistically rigorous, probabilistic validation metric that naturally controls the false discovery rate and allows astronomers to select candidate lists with a known confidence level, optimizing follow-up observations.

ideas.title: A Continuum Model for Stellar Populations Using Ecological Niche Theory
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a two-branch alpha-abundance dichotomy in Galactic chemical evolution by drawing an analogy from community ecology. In ecology, the shift from discrete classification of species guilds to modeling continua in niche space provides a parallel solution. We adapt this by conceptualizing the galactic chemical environment—parameters like local gas density, star formation efficiency, and merger/accretion history—as a multidimensional niche space. Stellar populations are then modeled not as belonging to one of two distinct sequences, but as having a probability distribution of abundances across this continuum of formation conditions. Using statistical ordination and gradient analysis techniques from ecology, the research would aim to identify the dominant environmental drivers and their continuous interplay, explaining the observed abundance scatter as a natural outcome of a continuum of formation paths rather than a binary dichotomy.

ideas.title: Bayesian Action-Distribution Clustering for Robust Stellar Stream Detection
ideas.description: This approach develops a probabilistic clustering framework that explicitly accounts for the uncertainties and incompleteness of Galactic survey data. It employs a Bayesian hierarchical model to infer the full phase-space distribution function of stars, marginalizing over measurement errors in proper motions, parallaxes, and radial velocities. The method then identifies statistically significant clusters in the space of orbital actions (or other integrals of motion) by analyzing the posterior distributions, rather than point estimates. This enables the robust detection of stellar streams and groups even with noisy, partial data, separating true dynamical structures from spurious associations caused by observational errors.

ideas.title: Adapting Coherent Structure Detection from Fluid Dynamics to Stellar Phase Space
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution for identifying Lagrangian Coherent Structures (LCS) from fluid dynamics to the problem of finding stellar streams and groups in orbital action space. In fluids, coherent structures like transport barriers are found by calculating Finite-Time Lyapunov Exponent (FTLE) fields from flow velocity data; ridges in this field cluster trajectories with similar dynamical histories. The adaptation treats the Milky Way's gravitational potential as a flow field and computes a stellar analog of FTLEs through numerical orbit integration. The resulting 'Orbital Lyapunov Exponent' field will reveal ridges that act as phase-space boundaries, within which stars are dynamically clustered. This method provides a rigorous, equation-of-motion-based technique to identify tidal streams, globular cluster debris, and other coherent stellar groups directly from their fundamental dynamical properties in phase space.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Signal Detection Networks for Faint X-ray Source Finding
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where deep learning models detect faint seismic signals in noisy time-series data, to the problem of finding faint X-ray sources. In seismology, convolutional and recurrent neural networks are applied to spectrograms to identify subtle, extended seismic events. Adapted to X-ray astronomy, a hybrid Convolutional-Recurrent Neural Network would be trained to detect faint, extended, or variable sources by analyzing X-ray data not just as static images, but as spatial-temporal photon events or sequences, learning to distinguish complex source signatures from the intricate background noise of X-ray detectors.

ideas.title: Bayesian Model-Averaging Framework for Prior-Robust Validation of Low-Significance Sources
ideas.description: This system robustly validates low-significance X-ray source candidates by directly confronting the fundamental subjectivity in Bayesian priors and models. It integrates a suite of competing prior distributions and likelihood models, each representing a different plausible astrophysical or instrumental hypothesis, into a formal model-averaging framework. The final validation metric is a Bayesian Model Averaging posterior probability, which marginalizes over this ensemble of models, ensuring the conclusion is not dependent on any single, possibly misspecified, assumption. This produces a more defensible and quantitatively robust assessment of source reality, complete with an explicit measure of uncertainty stemming from model choice.

ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from the computational biochemistry field, specifically the Foldit protein folding game, to the problem of classifying optical counterparts for X-ray sources. Foldit successfully gamified complex 3D pattern recognition, leveraging human problem-solving skills to achieve results that eluded automated systems. The adapted research idea involves developing a similar interactive, game-like citizen science platform where volunteers examine aligned astronomical images. Players would manipulate and match X-ray source positions with potential optical/IR counterparts, using game mechanics like points, levels, and collaboration to classify source types (e.g., AGN, star, galaxy cluster) and flag interesting or anomalous associations for professional follow-up, thereby combining human visual intuition with scalable crowd-sourcing.

ideas.title: Adaptive Ultra-Deep Study: Conditional Investment in Verified Variability
ideas.description: This approach applies the full 2 Ms exposure time to perform a time-domain study of a single, high-value variable source, but with a built-in risk mitigation protocol. The observation is split into a short, intensive characterization segment (e.g., 100-200 ks) followed by a major decision point. Automated and human-reviewed analysis of this initial data confirms the source's variability nature, excludes contaminants, and assesses scientific promise against pre-defined criteria. Based on this assessment, the mission either continues on the confirmed-primary target or executes a pre-approved pivot to a backup, equally compelling variable source, thus guaranteeing the deep exposure is spent on a target of validated interest.

ideas.title: Scalable Multi-Fidelity Sparse Gaussian Processes for Continental Gradient Mapping
ideas.description: This redesigned system overcomes the computational bottleneck of standard GPs by implementing a sparse variational inference framework, enabling the analysis of massive spatial datasets. It incorporates a multi-fidelity model that fuses precise but scarce field measurements with dense, spatially exhaustive remote sensing layers (e.g., spectral indices, topography) as a correlated low-fidelity process. This approach maintains the non-parametric flexibility and uncertainty quantification of GPs while achieving tractable computation for high-resolution, large-area ecological gradient maps. The result is a robust, scalable method for producing continuous abundance surfaces with quantified uncertainty from local to continental scales.

ideas.title: An Active Learning Loop Integrating Crowd-Sourced Disagreement with Expert-Vetted Machine Classification
ideas.description: This redesigned system frames citizen scientist classifications not as definitive labels, but as probabilistic inputs to a hierarchical Bayesian model that estimates the true source class and the reliability of each volunteer. The platform identifies sources with high crowd disagreement and automatically elevates them for rapid expert astronomer review via a separate interface. These expert-vetted labels then train a supervised machine learning classifier, whose confidence scores are used to strategically feed new, challenging sources back to the citizen scientists. This creates a continuous cycle where human uncertainty directly fuels the improvement of an automated classifier, ensuring robust final catalogs while efficiently using both crowd and expert labor.

ideas.title: Adapting Kriging with External Drift from Mining Geostatistics for Ecological Gradient Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts the geostatistical method of Kriging with External Drift (KED), a specialized Gaussian Process regression technique from mining and resource estimation, to the problem of mapping ecological abundance gradients. In the analogous mining problem, sparse drill-hole data for ore grade is interpolated using a Gaussian Process (ordinary kriging) that is explicitly constrained by a secondary, densely sampled dataset (like a geophysical survey) which defines a non-stationary mean trend or 'external drift.' This produces more accurate resource models. Adapted to ecology, the method would use non-parametric Gaussian Process regression to map a target gradient (e.g., soil nutrient concentration, species density) from sparse field samples. The key adaptation is to formally incorporate a densely sampled, spatially continuous remote sensing layer (e.g., NDVI, surface temperature, topographic wetness index) as the external drift variable within the GP's covariance structure. This produces a gradient map that is statistically rigorous, honors the point measurements, and is informed by the landscape-scale pattern evident in the remote sensing data, effectively transferring the solution from resource geology to spatial ecology.
